items.author: joaofelipefaria
items.content:
Salut, développeur ! Prêt à plonger dans un projet qui n'est pas seulement une expérience d'apprentissage, mais aussi un environnement pratique pour le développement full-stack avec et ? Laissez-moi vous présenter le , un système modulaire parfait pour gérer les données cartographiques et les métadonnées, tout en vous offrant la flexibilité d'explorer plusieurs frameworks et outils.

Qu'est-ce que le Projet MapData ?

Le est avant tout un environnement de travail de base pour les développeurs full-stack. Il repose sur une architecture de microservices avec une API backend, une application frontend Single Page Application (SPA) et des utilitaires pour gérer la base de données et l'environnement. Que vous soyez un développeur expérimenté ou débutant, ce projet est une excellente opportunité pour explorer les technologies modernes.

Voici ce qui est inclus :

  • : Une API RESTful pour gérer les données cartographiques et les métadonnées.
  • : Une application frontend élégante basée sur Angular pour l'interaction utilisateur.
  • : Gère la création et la maintenance du schéma de la base de données.
  • : Une version frontend légère utilisant HTML, CSS et JavaScript pur.
  • mapdata-automations-python : Scripts Python pour automatiser et gérer l'environnement.

Et le meilleur ? Vous pouvez ! Vous voulez essayer au lieu de ? au lieu de ? Allez-y ! Le projet est conçu pour être flexible.

La Technologie Derrière la Magie

  • : Le frontend SPA est construit avec Angular, un framework puissant pour créer des applications web dynamiques et réactives.
  • : Tout fonctionne dans des conteneurs, ce qui simplifie le déploiement et la gestion de l'environnement.
  • : Les scripts d'automatisation sont écrits en Python, une langue polyvalente idéale pour les tâches d'automatisation.
  • : Pour ceux qui aiment la simplicité, le composant est une version frontend pure en HTML/JS.

items.title: What are Filter Keys on Windows 11?
items.author: Vigneshwaran Vijayakumar
items.content:
Just like Sticky Keys, Filter Keys on Windows 11 is an useful accessibility feature. It is essential for users who has difficulty typing due to Hand Tremors or Accidental Key Presses. When Filter Keys Feature is enabled on Windows 11, Windows 11 will make sure the keyboard ignores . This will make it easier for people with . In this interesting article, let us check out in detail about Filter Keys on Windows 11. Let’s get Started!

Multiple Ways to Enable Filter Keys on Windows 11

It is very easy to turn on Filter keys on Windows 11. You can either use the or you can enable it using the .

Method 1 – Enable Filter Keys via the Keyboard Shortcut

This method is the quickest method to activate filter keys on Windows 11.

  • In the simple method, we can use the Right Shift Key to Enable this Feature. Press and hold Right Shift Key for 8 Seconds. This will trigger the Filter Keys pop up.
  • The pop up will prompt “ Do you want to turn on Filter Keys? “, click to turn on Filter Keys on Windows 11.

Method 2 – Use Windows Settings and Enable Filter Keys

  • Go to the using the keyboard shortcut Win Key + I.
  • Once opens, from the pane, click on .
  • Under , click on .
  • You can toggle the Filter Keys switch to to activate Filter Keys on Windows 11.

Method 3 – Turn on Filter Keys via the Control Panel

In this method, we will use the Control Panel to access the Filter Keys on Windows 11.

  • Go to the and open .
  • In the , switch the view to .
  • Click .
  • To adjust the , click “ Make the keyboard easier to use “.
  • Under , enable Turn on Filter Keys checkbox. Finally, click .

Setup Filter Keys on Windows 11

Once you enable this feature on Windows 11, you can configure various settings related to it. You can either use the or . We will discuss each section in detail here.

  • Click on .
  • Under , you can find various options such as Turn on Filter Keys when Right Shift Key pressed for 8 seconds. Windows can also display a warning message when turning a setting on and make a sound when turning a setting on or off.
  • Under Filter options, you can either turn on Bounce Keys or Repeat Keys & Slow Keys. You can also set the duration of these keys.

Note: You can test these settings by typing the text in the provided text box.

Configure Filter Keys using the Windows Settings

  • In the Windows Settings, open .
  • Under this , you can configure various settings related to the Filter Keys on Windows 11. The Settings include Keyboard Shortcut for Filter Keys , Show the Filter Keys icon on the taskbar , Beep when keys are pressed or accepted , , Ignore Unintended Keystrokes , Ignore repeated Keystrokes and the related .

Essential Features of Filter Keys on Windows 11

Filter Keys set the sensitivity of the keyboard so it can ignore brief or repeated keystrokes. You can configure , , and . All of these are related to the Filter Keys.

Slow Keys – Ignore Quick Keystrokes

If Slow Keys are enabled within the Filter Keys, then Windows 11 requires a key to be held down for a certain duration before it registers as an input. Users can easily and it will help Windows to .

Bounce Keys – Ignore Unintended Keystrokes

Enabling Bounce keys on Windows 11 will prevent accidental repeated keystrokes. This is an useful feature especially for users with or . Just like Slow Keys, we can set the time durataion for Bounce Keys as well. The Time Ranges from .

Repeat Keys – Ignore Repeated Keystrokes

Usually, when we hold any key, it starts repeating after a short delay. For example, When we press and hold the right arrow in a text editor over a paragraph, then cursor will start moving towards the right crossing one letter after another. However, if we enable Repeat Keys within Filter Keys, then we can control how quickly a key repeats when held down. Here, you can set the “ Wait before accepting the first repeated keystroke “, and “ Wait before accepting subsequent repeated keystrokes “.

How to Disable “Do you want to turn on Filter Keys” pop up on Windows 11?

Though Filter Keys on Windows 11 is an useful feature, it can be a bit inconvenient when we receive the pop up “ Do you want to turn on Filter Keys? ” Have you ever wondered why does this dialog box pop up? We might have faced this situation mostly while Gaming, where we may be pressing the Right Shift Key for a long time, like using Nitrous in a Car Game. If it is a real-world game, then we might even lose the game.

Why does this pop up appears on Windows 11?

The reason why this dialog box pops up is that if the option is enabled, pressing Right Shift Key continously for 8 Seconds will trigger this pop up. We might be using Nitrous in the game, however, the system may mistake it for the Filter Keys.

  • Now, we have the answer, let’s check out How to turn off “Do you want to turn on Filter Keys?” pop up on Windows 11.
  • Open using the shortcut Win Key + I.
  • From the left pane, click on .
  • Open from .
  • Here, you can find the option “ Keyboard Shortcut for Filter Keys “. If you have received the pop up, then this option would have been enabled. Toggle the switch to OFF to turn off Keyboard Shortcut for Filter Keys.

The above article provides detailed information on What is Filter Keys, How to use it, and How to Turn off “ Do you want to Turn on Filter Keys ” pop up in Windows 11? Filter Keys is a useful Accessibility Feature on Windows 11 and it can be user-friendly for individuals with . We appreciate Microsoft for Adding this Feature on Windows Operating Systems. If you have any queries, kindly let us know in the comment section. For more interesting articles, stay tuned to . Happy Computing! Peace out!

items.title: Unpacking Go Channels: A Peek Under the Hood
items.author: Shrijith Venkatramana
items.content:
Hi there! I'm Shrijith Venkatrama, founder of Hexmos. Right now, I’m building LiveAPI, a tool that makes generating API docs from your code ridiculously easy.

If you’ve messed around with Go, you’ve probably bumped into channels.

They’re the secret sauce for concurrency, letting goroutines talk to each other without tripping over themselves.

But what’s really going on when you or send a value with ?

This post is all about tearing apart Go channels to see how they work internally. Buckle up—we’re going deep!

We’ll break it down into : what channels are, their internal structure, how sending and receiving works, and some gotchas to watch out for.

Along the way, you’ll get code, diagrams, and a few “aha!” moments (hopefully!).

What Are Channels, Anyway?

Channels in Go are like pipes.

You shove data in one end, and it pops out the other—except they’re built for concurrency, so they’re safe for goroutines to use without extra locks.

Think of them as a queue with some magic sprinkled on top.

You create a channel with :

  • block until both a sender and receiver are ready.
  • let you send data up to their capacity before blocking.

Under the hood, channels are implemented in the Go runtime, written in C and assembly (yep, low-level stuff).

The key player is a struct called , defined in the Go source code ().

It’s the backbone of every channel you create. Let’s see what makes it tick.

The struct is where the magic lives. Here’s a simplified version of it from the Go runtime:

  • : How many items are sitting in the buffer right now.
  • : The total capacity (0 for unbuffered channels).
  • : A circular buffer (think ring queue) holding the data.
  • : Indices tracking where to send or receive next.
  • : Queues of goroutines waiting to act—super important for blocking.

Here’s a diagram of a buffered channel with capacity 3:

  • Channel Buffer Section (Cap: 3)

    • The channel currently holds (full buffer).
    • No empty slots are available.
    • A sender () is trying to send a new item but is because the buffer is .
    • The sender must wait until a receiver takes an item, freeing space.
    • A receiver () is to receive.
    • When it receives , the buffer shifts left, making room for another sender.

For an unbuffered channel (), there’s no . Instead, data moves directly from sender to receiver, and the runtime uses and to pair them up.

: The struct is a mix of a queue and a coordinator, juggling data and goroutines like a pro.

Sending and Receiving—How It Works

Let’s get into the nitty-gritty of sending () and receiving ().

The runtime handles these operations differently depending on whether the channel is buffered or unbuffered.

With an unbuffered channel, it’s a handshake. The sender blocks until a receiver is ready, and vice versa. Here’s an example:

  1. Sender calls , checks .
  2. If a receiver’s waiting, data moves directly (no buffer), and both goroutines wake up.
  3. If not, the sender joins and sleeps.
  4. Receiver does the reverse with .

Buffered channels are more chill. You can send until the buffer’s full. Here’s a quick demo:

  1. If there’s space (), adds the value to at , bumps , and updates .
  2. If full, sender joins and waits.
  3. Receiver pulls from , shifts the queue, and wakes a sender if isn’t empty.

Here’s a table comparing the two:

Direct goroutine-to-goroutine

: Buffered channels use a queue, unbuffered ones play matchmaker. The runtime’s job is to keep it smooth and safe.

Channels are awesome, but they’ve got quirks. Here’s what to watch out for and some neat tricks.

Closing a channel () sets to 1. Receivers can still drain the buffer, but sending panics. Check it out:

The block is your friend for juggling multiple channels:

It picks the first ready case—or if nothing’s ready.

  • Sending to a closed channel? Panic.
  • Closing a closed channel? Panic.
  • Nil channel in ? Ignored (handy for toggling).

: Channels are powerful, but test edge cases—closing, nil, and full buffers can bite.

Go channels are a concurrency superpower, blending queues, locks, and goroutine scheduling into one neat package.

The struct ties it all together, and the runtime makes it feel effortless.

Whether you’re syncing goroutines or pipelining data, understanding the internals helps you wield them like a pro.

Want to dig deeper? Check out these resources:

Got questions or cool channel tricks? Drop a comment—I’d love to hear them!

items.title: Shopify Pricing Hacks: How to Use Discounts to Increase Repeat Purchases
items.author: James Kujawa
items.content:
Most Shopify merchants lose money on discounts because they use them incorrectly. Flat discounts attract one-time buyers, but they don’t drive repeat purchases or increase AOV (Average Order Value).

The key? Use structured, psychology-driven discounts that make customers spend more—both now and in the future.

🚨 Why Traditional Discounts Fail

Many merchants assume that lower prices = more sales, but that’s not true.

❌ Flat 10% or 20% discounts train customers to wait for sales instead of buying now.

❌ One-time coupon codes don’t guarantee higher cart values.

❌ Overusing discounts cuts into profits without building long-term loyalty.

Instead, you need discount strategies that:

✔ Increase order size (AOV) instantly

✔ Encourage repeat purchases without relying on coupons

✔ Make discounts feel exclusive, not expected

Here’s how to do it the right way.

🎯 Shopify Discount Tactics That Drive Repeat Purchases

🔹 1. Buy X for $Y (Bundle Deals That Make Customers Spend More)

👉 Buy Any 3 Items from the Winter Collection for $120 (instead of $150) Encourages bulk purchases while giving controlled discounts.

✅ Can apply to an entire collection or a specific product group (not mix-and-match at random).

🔹 2. Conditional Discounts (Reward High-Spending Customers)

👉 Get 20% Off on Hoodies Collection If Your Cart Total Exceeds $250 Increases cart value strategically instead of discounting everything.

✅ Applies to specific collections or categories based on cart value.

🔹 3. Volume Discounts (Encourage Bulk Purchases)

👉 Buy 5 T-shirts, Get 15% Off; Buy 10, Get 20% Off Boosts AOV by making customers buy more per order.

✅ Discounts increase in tiers, making higher purchases more attractive.

🔹 4. Enhanced Buy X Get Y (BOGO++)

👉 Buy Any 3 Accessories, Get 1 Free Moves inventory strategically while increasing order value.

✅ Can be applied to a specific product list instead of allowing storewide free items.

🔹 5. Shipping Discounts (Push Customers to Higher Spending Brackets)

👉 Free Shipping on Orders Over $300 (Standard Shipping Costs $12 Otherwise) Customers will add extra items to unlock free shipping.

✅ Works best for high-ticket products where shipping is expensive.

🚀 The Best Way to Implement These Discounts

Shopify’s default discount system can’t handle these advanced strategies—you need a flexible discounting engine.

🔹 Apps like allow merchants to:

✔ Set up Buy X for $Y rules on entire collections or specific product groups

✔ Offer conditional discounts based on cart value and product rules

✔ Run BOGO++ and volume discounts that don’t interfere with other deals

🔥 Stop Using Discounts Wrong—Start Scaling with Smart Pricing

Instead of giving discounts blindly, use them as a revenue weapon—driving higher AOV and securing repeat customers without margin loss.

📌 Optimize your discounts today—watch your sales explode! 🚀

items.title: Understanding the Single Responsibility Principle in S.O.L.I.D
items.author: RakibRahman
items.content:
1. What is the S.O.L.I.D Principle?
  2. Real-World Analogy of SRP
  3. Code Example
    • What breaks SRP
      • 1. Not adhering to DRY principle
      • 2. Missing KISS principle
    • How to achieve SRP
      • 1. Adhere to the DRY Principle
      • 2. Follow the KISS Principle

    Writing flexible and scalable code is hard, but the principles help us achieve that. At first glance, may seem daunting, and applying it correctly takes time and practice. However, with proper theoretical knowledge and hands-on experience, mastering these principles becomes achievable.

    I’m writing this blog to:

    1. Document my learning journey – so I (and others) can revisit and refresh our understanding.
    2. – As I gain more insights, I’ll update and refine my explanations.

    This will be a , where each blog dives deep into one principle. Let’s begin with the first one: Single Responsibility Principle (SRP)

    What is the S.O.L.I.D Principle?

    SOLID is an acronym for the first five object-oriented design (OOD) principles, introduced by Robert C. Martin (Uncle Bob) in the early 2000s. These principles help developers create software that is:

    • -> Easy to modify and extend.
    • –> Clear and readable structure.
    • –> Adapts to changes without breaking.
    • –> Reduces complexity as the application grows.

      The Single Responsibility Principle (SRP) states that:

      A class or module should have only one reason to change, meaning it should have only one job or responsibility.

      • One class = One primary job or responsibility.
      • If a class does too much, it becomes harder to maintain.
      • Changes in one functionality shouldn’t risk breaking unrelated features.

      Violating often leads to tight coupling, which makes code hard to test and maintain.

      Real-World Analogy of SRP

      Let’s try to understand SRP with a real-world scenario. Think of a supershop where:

      • The cashier only deals with cash (doesn’t help customers find products).
      • The salesman only helps customers locate desired products (doesn’t manage cash).
      • The cleaner only cleans (doesn’t assist with product searches or cash handling).

      The supershop works efficiently because each expert focuses on their responsiblity. Changes to one role do not disrupt others.

      Code Should Work the Same Way! Just as you wouldn't expect a cleaner to handle billing, a class or module shouldn't mix unrelated responsibilities.

      Enough theoretical knowledge. Let's see in action. I'll use Go(Golang) to demonstrate the , but the following example should also apply to other languages.

      Let’s look at a common scenario where SRP is violated. In this example, the struct handles both saving data to the database and sending emails.

      type User struct {
    name  string
    email string
}

// Violation of SRP. It handles both database operations and email sending.
func (u *User) Save() error {
    // Logic to save user to the database
    fmt.Printf("Saving user %s to database\n", u.name)

    // Logic to send email
    u.SendEmail(u.email, "Welcome to our community")
    return nil
}

func (u *User) SendEmail(email, message string) {
    // Email sending logic
    fmt.Println(message, email)
}

func main() {
    user := User{name: "Rakib", email: "rakib@gmail.com"}
    user.Save()
}
      • The method handles both database and email operations, which violates .
      • Additionally, we can’t reuse database/email-related logic because it’s tightly coupled to the method.

      Now, let’s refactor the code to adhere to . We’ll separate the responsibilities into dedicated methods.

      type User struct {
    name  string
    email string
}

// DatabaseService handles database operations - single responsibility
type DatabaseService struct{}

func (db *DatabaseService) SaveUser(user *User) {
    // Logic to save user to the database
    fmt.Println("Saving user to database", user.name)
}

// EmailService handles email-related operations - single responsibility
type EmailService struct{}

func (es *EmailService) SendEmail(user *User, message string) {
    // Logic to send an email
    fmt.Println(message, user.email)
}

func main() {
    user := User{name: "Rakib", email: "rakib@gmail.com"}

    dbService := &DatabaseService{}
    emailService := &EmailService{}

    dbService.SaveUser(&user)
    emailService.SendEmail(&user, "Welcome to our community")
}

      • Now, we’ve assigned each responsibility to a separate module:

        • handles database operations.
        • handles email-related operations.

      • The and methods are not tightly coupled, can be reused, and changing one’s logic does not disrupt the other.

      Here are some common scenarios where is broken:

      1. Not adhering to principle.

      • Duplicate functionality in your code violates . If you copy-paste the same logic into multiple places, it often indicates scattered responsibilities and a lack of a single source of truth. 
         // Duplicate validation logic in two places  
func CreateUser(name string, email string) error {
    if len(name) == 0 {
        return errors.New("name cannot be empty")
    }
    // ... rest of the function  
}

func UpdateUser(name string, email string) error {
    if len(name) == 0 {
        return errors.New("name cannot be empty")
    }
    // ... rest of the function  
}
      • In the above example, the and methods use the same logic for validating the name. If we need to update the logic, we have to do it manually in each method.
      • When a class tries to do too much, it becomes overly complex and difficult to understand. The level of abstraction is missing. 
        type Report struct {}

func (r *Report) Generate() string { /* ... */ }
func (r *Report) SaveToFile() error { /* ... */ }
func (r *Report) SendEmail() error { /* ... */ }
      • Here, the struct handles generating reports, saving them to files, and sending emails—all in one place.
      • When a class is tightly coupled with other classes or modules, it becomes harder to modify one without affecting others. This coupling often arises when a class takes on responsibilities that don't belong to it. 
      type Order struct {
    DB *sql.DB
}

func (o *Order) Process() error {
    // Business logic + direct database access  
    _, err := o.DB.Exec("INSERT INTO orders...")
    return err
}
      • The struct is directly responsible for both business logic and database operations, making it tightly coupled with the database.

      To adhere to the Single Responsibility Principle, address the issues outlined above with these strategies:

      1. Adhere to the DRY Principle

      • Centralize shared functionality into a single module or function. For example, extract the validation logic into a dedicated function to avoid duplication. 
      func ValidateName(name string) error {
    if len(name) == 0 {
        return errors.New("name cannot be empty")
    }
    return nil
}

func CreateUser(name string, email string) error {
    if err := ValidateName(name); err != nil {
        return err
    }
    // ... rest of the function  
}

func UpdateUser(name string, email string) error {
    if err := ValidateName(name); err != nil {
        return err
    }
    // ... rest of the function  
}

      2. Follow the KISS Principle

      • Keep classes simple and focused. Each class should have . Split the struct into smaller, focused structs. 
      type ReportGenerator struct{}

func (r *ReportGenerator) Generate() string {
    // Logic for generating report
    return "Generated Report"
}

type FileSaver struct{}

func (f *FileSaver) SaveToFile(content string) error {
    // Logic for saving to file
    return nil
}

type EmailSender struct{}

func (e *EmailSender) SendEmail(content string) error {
    // Logic for sending email
    return nil
}
      • Each struct now has a single responsibility: generating reports, saving files, or sending emails.
      • Use composition to delegate responsibilities to other classes. For instance, separate the business logic from database operations by injecting a repository or service. 
        type OrderRepository struct {
    DB *sql.DB
}

func (r *OrderRepository) Save(orderData map[string]interface{}) error {
    // Database-specific logic
    _, err := r.DB.Exec("INSERT INTO orders...", orderData)
    return err
}

type Order struct {
    Repository *OrderRepository
}

func (o *Order) Process(orderData map[string]interface{}) error {
    // Business logic only
    return o.Repository.Save(orderData)
}
      • Here, the struct delegates database operations to the , reducing coupling and adhering to SRP.
      • -> The and can be reused across different parts of the application, reducing duplication.
      • -> Responsibilities are isolated, so it’s easier to pinpoint issues.
      • -> Each class or module has a clear purpose and can be tested independently. It allows for without mocking unrelated logic.
      • -> The code is modular, providing greater readability.
      • -> Changing one module does not cause unintended side effects in other modules. For example, if the email-sending logic changes (e.g., switching from SMTP to an external service), only the needs modification, leaving the unaffected.
      • -> often leads to better abstraction, which is a key concept in good software design.

      Simply put, implementing greatly improves code quality. It makes the code easier to maintain, test, and scale. Always try to implement in your projects to improve code quality. Stay tuned for the next blog in the series, which will cover the Open/Closed Principle (OCP).

      That’s all for today! Thank you for reading, and don’t forget to connect with me on LinkedIn to get updates.

      If you have any questions or feedback, please leave a comment below!

items.title: vs-code commands to create a dotnet project

items.content:
I will walk you through the steps to create a console application using Visual Studio Code (VS Code), manage projects within a solution, add packages like Entity Framework, and explore additional features that enhance your development experience.

Before you begin, ensure you have the following installed:

  • : Download and install the .NET SDK from the .NET website.
  • : Install the C# Dev Kit extension from the Extensions Marketplace in VS Code.

Step 1: List Available Project Templates

To see a list of available project templates, you can use the following command:

This command will display all the templates available for creating different types of projects, including console applications, web applications, class libraries.. You will take the names that are in the short name column.

Step 2: Create a New Console Application

  1. : Launch VS Code.
  2. Open the Integrated Terminal: You can open the terminal by selecting from the top menu and then , or by using the shortcut (backtick).
  3. Create a New Console Application: In the terminal, navigate to the directory where you want to create your project. Use the following command to create a new console application: 
   dotnet new console  MyConsoleApp

This command creates a new folder named containing the console application.

  1. Navigate to the Project Directory:

Step 3: Create a Solution and Add the Project

  1. : If you want to manage multiple projects, you can create a solution. Navigate to the parent directory where you want to create the solution and run:

Navigate back to the parent Directory:

   dotnet new sln  MySolution

This creates a new solution file named .

  1. Add the Console Application to the Solution: Navigate back to the project directory and add the console application to the solution: 
   dotnet sln add MyConsoleApp/MyConsoleApp.csproj

Step 4: Add a Package to the Project

To add a package, such as Entity Framework Core, you can use the command. Here’s how to do it:

  1. Install Entity Framework Core: In the terminal, run the following command: 
   dotnet add package Microsoft.EntityFrameworkCore

This command will add the Entity Framework Core package to your project.

  1. Install Additional Packages: If you need to use a database provider (e.g., SQL Server), you can install the corresponding package: 
   dotnet add package Microsoft.EntityFrameworkCore.SqlServer

Open the file in the directory and modify it to include some basic functionality. For example, you can add code to connect to a database using Entity Framework Core:

Step 6: Run the Application

To run your console application, use the following command in the terminal:

This command will build and execute your application, displaying the output in the terminal.

For just building the project and not running it, just use:

You can debug your console application in VS Code:

If you are using Git for version control, you can easily initialize a Git repository for your console application and manage your code changes:

  1. Initialize a Git Repository: In the terminal, run the following command in your project directory:
  1. Add Files to the Repository: Stage your files for the initial commit:
  1. : Commit the staged files with a message: 
   git commit
  1. Connect to a Remote Repository: If you want to push your code to a remote repository (e.g., GitHub), you can add a remote URL: 
   git remote add origin https://github.com/username/repository.git
  1. : Push your local commits to the remote repository:

4. Using Configuration Files

You can manage application settings using configuration files. For example, you can create an file to store configuration settings for your application.

  1. : In the project directory, create a new file named and add your configuration settings:
  1. Read Configuration in Your Application: You can read the configuration settings in your file using the Microsoft.Extensions.Configuration package. First, add the package: 
   dotnet add package Microsoft.Extensions.Configuration
   dotnet add package Microsoft.Extensions.Configuration.Json

Then, modify your to read the configuration:

5. Testing Your Application

You can add unit tests to your console application to ensure that your code behaves as expected. Here’s how to create a test project:

  1. : Navigate to the parent directory of your console application and run: 
   dotnet new nunit  MyConsoleApp.Tests
  1. Add the Test Project to the Solution: 
   dotnet sln add MyConsoleApp.Tests/MyConsoleApp.Tests.csproj
  1. Add a Reference to the Console Application: 
   dotnet add MyConsoleApp.Tests/MyConsoleApp.Tests.csproj reference MyConsoleApp/MyConsoleApp.csproj

  1. : Open the file in the test project and write your tests using the xUnit framework.

  2. : You can run your tests using the following command:

That's how we use vs-code to work with dotnet application.

items.title: And When We Thought We Had Seen It All… Here Comes the Right Dev Tool for Our Toolbox
items.author: Luca Visciola
items.content:
The Developer's Dilemma: Passwords, Security, and Chaos

Let's be honest: as developers, we've all been there. You're debugging an urgent issue, and suddenly—boom—someone pings you a password over Slack, plain text. Or worse, you get an email that starts with "Here are the credentials:" followed by a heart-stopping list of access keys, databases, and (probably) the WiFi password of the sender’s home network.

We know it’s wrong. We know it's a bad practice. But we do it anyway, because who has time to set up proper secure channels when production is on fire?

Enter —the tool we didn’t know we needed, but now can’t ignore.

SharePassword: The "WeTransfer" for Secrets

SharePassword is like that friend who tells you to stop storing your API keys in . It simplifies the secure sharing of sensitive information using advanced encryption, ensuring that even if a link falls into the wrong hands, it remains as useful as a floppy disk in 2025.

Here’s the magic behind it:

  • : Every password you share is encrypted with a 256-bit key, making it nearly impossible for anyone to decrypt it without authorization. (If someone does, they’re probably a time traveler.)

  • : Generate a secure link with a single click, send it, and rest easy knowing it will self-destruct after use—just like those Mission: Impossible messages (but without the dramatic explosions).

  • No storage of sensitive data: Even the platform’s developers couldn’t access your secrets if they tried. (No peeking temptations.)

Why Does This Matter? (Or: "Why Should I Care?")

Matteo Biasi, the mind behind SharePassword, had a wake-up call when he realized how often passwords were sent unprotected. If even tech-savvy people are doing it wrong, imagine the state of password sharing among non-developers. (Spoiler: it’s horrifying.)

The philosophy here is simple: let’s not make security an afterthought. SharePassword isn’t just about making encryption accessible—it’s about nudging us toward better habits. If tools like this become part of our daily workflow, we’re one step closer to a world where “admin/admin” is no longer a valid login.

The Academic Angle: Science Backs This Up

, a professor at the Free University of Bolzano, highlights how modern technology can recognize and authenticate users based on their behavior. We’re entering an era where security isn't just about strong passwords, but about we interact with systems. SharePassword fits into this evolving landscape, ensuring that while we wait for the passwordless future, we at least share secrets responsibly.

Final Thoughts: Put This in Your Dev Toolbox

We developers love discovering the "right tool for the job." And while we have IDEs that autocomplete our code, frameworks that do the heavy lifting, and AI that helps us debug (or at least hallucinate solutions), we still lack common sense when it comes to security.

So, next time you’re about to send a password over Slack, stop. Use instead. Your future self (and your company’s security team) will thank you.

What do you think? Have you ever received (or sent—be honest) passwords in a ridiculously insecure way?

items.title: 90 Days of DevOps - Day 1: What is DevOps and why should we learn it?
items.author: ByteMotive Blog
items.content:
Hello everyone, welcome to your one stop solution for all tech problems BYTEMOTIVE. So, from today we are going to start a new series of 90 days of DevOps for which I will be following Michel Cade’s 90 Days of DevOps roadmap. Are you ready to accept this challenge to start learning with me or honestly speaking make me motivated in this whole journey filled with excitement, energy, codes and not our favorite bugs!!!

What is DevOps? A Beginner’s Guide

Before digging deep into this challenge let me introduce with DevOps (if you know already doesn’t matter just read it…).Have you ever wondered that how these big tech companies like Netflix, Amazon, or Google push updates without breaking things. If your answer is no then you might pay more attention on Netflix And Chill rather than studying DevOps. But don’t worry I will explain you because that’s the reason we exist. DevOps is a combination of Development (Dev) and Operations (Ops) that focuses on automation, collaboration, and continuous improvement. It helps teams build, test, and deploy software faster and more reliably. Instead of the traditional "Developer builds, Ops deploys" approach, DevOps unites both teams to work together, making the entire software lifecycle smoother and more efficient.

Why DevOps is a Game-Changer for Modern Tech

Till now we have understood that what the thing DevOps is, now dig dive to see what is it power? And why we call it Game Changer. Whether you're a developer, tester, or system admin:

✅ Faster Software Releases – No more waiting for weeks/months for an update. ✅ Better Collaboration – Dev and Ops teams work together instead of blaming each other.

✅ More Job Opportunities – DevOps engineers are in high demand with great salaries.

✅ Automation = Less Manual Work – Automate boring tasks and focus on real development.

How we are going to study all this in 90 days:

Now you must be thinking that I have understood that what is DevOps and why it is important but how will we cover this whole Giant in 90 days. So, you don’t need to worry because I have already prepared a 90-day strategy for you all readers you just need to follow us daily to learn a new topic and boost your skill. Afterall not only snapchat streaks matters Getting 90 days of DevOps streak will also sound good, trust me! Here is the roadmap for our rollercoaster journey of DevOps:

🔹 Phase 1: Foundations (Days 1-30) 🚀 ✅ Understanding DevOps Principles & Culture

✅ Linux & Shell Scripting Basics

✅ Git & Version Control Systems

✅ Networking Fundamentals for DevOps

🔹 Phase 2: Automation & CI/CD (Days 31-60) 🔄 ✅ Learning Infrastructure as Code (IaC) with Terraform

✅ Setting Up CI/CD Pipelines (Jenkins, GitHub Actions)

✅ Introduction to Containers: Docker & Kubernetes

✅ Deploying Applications with Kubernetes

🔹 Phase 3: Advanced DevOps & Cloud (Days 61-90) ☁️ ✅ Cloud Computing Basics (AWS, Azure, GCP)

✅ Monitoring & Logging (Prometheus, Grafana)

✅ Security & DevSecOps Best Practices

✅ Final Project: Building a Complete DevOps Pipeline

🔥 What’s Next? Now it’s getting boring because your attention limit might have reached to an end that’s why Tomorrow, we’ll dive into DevOps History & Culture—how this movement started and why it became a must-have skill. Stay tuned! 🚀

👀 Want to Follow Along? Drop a comment or share your thoughts—let’s build this DevOps journey together! 🎉

items.title: How to gain maximum out of AWS Compute Architectures?
items.author: VijayaNirmalaGopal
items.content:
Welcome to another essentials post with AWS Compute Architecture !! AWS Compute is about using provisioning EC2 servers for implementing functional aspects of any business requirement.

This post is about "How to get the maximum out of EC2 services but yet save huge on costs(given the workload can accommodate Spot interruptions) ?"

Need #1: Application hosted on AWS now meets with the business requirement but off late has gathered momentum and gets sudden demand with peaked requests. Existing design has to accommodate varying workload scenarios and should be capable of scaling up & down automatically

Autoscaling & Application Load Balancer together helps in making the compute capacity available for peak loads and ALB assures that traffic or requests are routed to healthy instances available inside an auto scaling group

Need #2: EC2 instance attribute types like OS/AMI or memory, storage, network parameters have to be rightly defined & used consistently

Launch Templates has to be used in order to retain multiple versions of Instance attributes combination(by using versions) and can be used for provisioning as needed along with Auto Scaling Group

Need #3: Capacity scales as needed during peak/off peak timelines with on-demand instances but we need best optimum compute usage to save costs

Creating EC2 Spot Fleet requests & spot instances(for workloads supporting interruptions & resuming) will make the implementation super successful & efficient by giving upto 90% discounts on compute usage

Need #4: Incoming traffic should be routed to all healthy instances that are available at any point in time

Definition & using of Target Groups will ensure the health check of the instance & redirects the requests from ALB as accordingly. Also helps in grouping mix of instance types like on-demand & spot definitions for a highly available & fault tolerant Application

For a focused explanation on spot & benefits rather an architectural blend, then visit Link

For AWS Compute architecture using all on-demand compute capacity please refer to Link

items.author: MD ARIFUL HAQUE
items.content:
2818. Apply Operations to Maximize Score

, , , , , ,

You are given an array of positive integers and an integer .

Initially, you start with a score of . You have to maximize your score by applying the following operation at most times:

  • Choose any subarray that you haven't chosen previously.
  • Choose an element of with the highest prime score. If multiple such elements exist, choose the one with the smallest index.

Here, denotes the subarray of starting at index and ending at the index , both ends being inclusive.

The of an integer is equal to the number of distinct prime factors of . For example, the prime score of is since .

Return the after applying at most operations.

Since the answer may be large, return it modulo .

  • nums = [8,3,9,3,8], k = 2
  • To get a score of 81, we can apply the following operations:
    • Choose subarray nums[2, ..., 2]. nums[2] is the only element in this subarray. Hence, we multiply the score by nums[2]. The score becomes 1 * 9 = 9.
    • Choose subarray nums[2, ..., 3]. Both nums[2] and nums[3] have a prime score of 1, but nums[2] has the smaller index. Hence, we multiply the score by nums[2]. The score becomes 9 * 9 = 81.
    • It can be proven that 81 is the highest score one can obtain.
  • nums = [19,12,14,6,10,18], k = 3
  • To get a score of 4788, we can apply the following operations:
    • Choose subarray nums[0, ..., 0]. nums[0] is the only element in this subarray. Hence, we multiply the score by nums[0]. The score becomes 1 * 19 = 19.
    • Choose subarray nums[5, ..., 5]. nums[5] is the only element in this subarray. Hence, we multiply the score by nums[5]. The score becomes 19 * 18 = 342.
    • Choose subarray nums[2, ..., 3]. Both nums[2] and nums[3] have a prime score of 2, but nums[2] has the smaller index. Hence, we multipy the score by nums[2]. The score becomes 342 * 14 = 4788.
    • It can be proven that 4788 is the highest score one can obtain.
  • 1 <= nums.length == n <= 10
  • 1 <= k <= min(n * (n + 1) / 2, 10)
  1. Calculate 's prime score by factoring in time.
  2. For each , find the nearest index on the left (if any) such that . if none is found, set to . Similarly, find the nearest index on the right (if any) such that . If none is found, set to .
  3. Use a monotonic stack to compute and .
  4. For each index , if left[i] + 1 <= l <= i <= r <= right[i] - 1, then is the maximum value in the range . For this particular , there are ranges[i] = (i - left[i]) * (right[i] - i) ranges where index will be chosen.
  5. Loop over all elements of by non-increasing prime score, each element will be chosen min(ranges[i], remainingK) times, where denotes the number of remaining operations. Therefore, the score will be multiplied by s[i]^min(ranges[i],remainingK).
  6. Use fast exponentiation to quickly calculate .

We need to maximize the score by applying operations on subarrays of the given array. Each operation involves selecting a subarray and multiplying the score by the element with the highest prime score in that subarray. The prime score of an element is the number of distinct prime factors it has.

  1. : Calculate the prime score for each element in the array. The prime score is the number of distinct prime factors of a number.
  2. Monotonic Stack for Boundaries: Use monotonic stacks to determine the left and right boundaries for each element where it is the maximum prime score element. This helps in identifying the valid subarrays where each element is the maximum.
  3. : For each element, compute the number of subarrays where it is the maximum element based on its left and right boundaries.
  4. : Sort the elements based on their value in descending order, followed by their prime score in descending order, and finally by their index in ascending order. This ensures that we prioritize elements with higher values and higher prime scores first.
  5. : Multiply the score by the highest possible values first, using fast exponentiation to handle large powers efficiently modulo .
  1. : Each element's prime score is determined by counting its distinct prime factors.
  2. : These are used to efficiently find the nearest left and right boundaries where the current element is the maximum in terms of prime score.
  3. : For each element, the number of valid subarrays where it is the maximum is calculated using the boundaries.
  4. : Elements are sorted by their value, prime score, and index to ensure the highest contributions are considered first.
  5. : The score is maximized by multiplying by the highest values first, using fast exponentiation to handle large powers efficiently. This ensures the solution is optimal and runs in a reasonable time frame.
