items.title: Fastify In-Depth: Speed, Performance, and Scalability Node.js Web Framework
items.author: Leapcell
items.content:
Fastify is an efficient and fast Node.js web framework designed to provide optimal performance. Although it is relatively new, it has won the favor of many developers due to its high performance and low overhead. Fastify offers a concise development experience, supports fast routing and a plugin architecture, making it easy for developers to build and expand applications. Referring to the Fastify official website, the following will introduce Fastify from multiple aspects.

1. Main Features and Principles

  1. : Fastify is one of the fastest web frameworks. Depending on the code complexity, it can handle up to 30,000 requests per second.
  2. : With the help of hooks, plugins, and decorators, Fastify is fully scalable.
  3. : Although not mandatory, it is recommended to use JSON Schema to validate routes and serialize outputs. Fastify will compile the schema into high-performance functions.
  4. : Choosing the best logger Pino almost eliminates the cost of logging.
  5. : This framework is highly expressive and convenient for developers' daily use without sacrificing performance and security.
  6. : It strives to maintain TypeScript type declaration files to support the growing TypeScript community.

2. Background of Its Birth

Before Fastify emerged, Express and Koa were two widely used frameworks in the Node.js field. Although they are popular and easy to use, their performance is not optimal when handling a large number of requests. Fastify aims to solve the following key issues:

  1. : By reducing the overhead of each request, it provides higher performance than existing frameworks and can handle more requests, which is crucial for building high-performance applications.
  2. : With the help of the plugin architecture and out-of-the-box functions (such as schema validation, logging, etc.), it provides an efficient and easy-to-use development environment, accelerating the development process and reducing potential errors.
  3. : It has built-in support for JSON Schema, which not only helps to validate client input but also ensures the data consistency and reliability of the application. This is a function that many frameworks do not integrate at the core level.
  4. : The plugin architecture allows developers to easily add new functions or integrate third-party libraries without having a significant impact on the overall performance of the application.

By solving the above problems, Fastify provides a powerful platform for developing high-performance, maintainable, and easy-to-develop Node.js applications, and has quickly gained attention and usage in the JavaScript community.

3. Realization of High Performance

The key to Fastify's high performance lies in its design and architectural choices, which help to minimize the overhead of each request:

  1. Efficient Routing Distribution: Fastify adopts a fast routing distribution mechanism. When a request comes in, it can quickly determine the processing function to be called, greatly reducing the request processing time.
  2. Precompiled Serialization: Before sending a response, Fastify uses precompiled serialization functions instead of dynamically serializing objects at runtime, thus speeding up the serialization of responses and sending them to clients.
  3. Schema-Driven Development: Fastify strongly recommends (and in some cases requires) using JSON Schema to define the input and output of routes. This not only helps to validate and document the API but also allows Fastify to precompile the validation logic, improving runtime efficiency.
  4. : Fastify's plugin architecture is designed efficiently, supporting code reuse and best practices while keeping the core framework lightweight, achieving a high degree of modularity and flexibility without sacrificing performance.
  5. : The built-in logging tool Pino is designed for speed and provides logging functions with extremely low overhead, which is crucial for maintaining application performance.
  6. : Fastify's design philosophy is to minimize the performance overhead of the abstraction layer. Even when using various abstractions and convenient functions, it can maintain high performance.

Through these design decisions and optimizations, Fastify can provide excellent performance and is an ideal choice for building efficient and responsive Node.js applications.

4. Installation and Usage

4.1 Install Fastify via npm

4.3 Generate a Skeleton Using the cli 

npm  fastify-cli
fastify generate newproject

4.4 Example of Handling Requests with JSON Schema and Hooks

5. Plugins, Decorators, Middleware, Hooks

In Fastify, plugins, decorators, middleware, and hooks are core concepts of the framework, each playing a different role:

Plugins are the main way to add functions, share code, or encapsulate logic in a Fastify application. A plugin can be a function that takes a Fastify instance, options, and a callback function as parameters. Plugins can register routes, add decorators, declare new hooks, and even encapsulate other plugins, and are used to build modular applications. Developers can use them to build reusable logic blocks and share them in different Fastify applications or different parts of the same application.

Decorators are used to extend the Fastify instance, request (Request), and reply (Reply) objects. By adding new methods or properties, developers can add custom functions or data and make them available in different parts of the application. For example, a decorator can be added to add a method for each request to access shared configuration data or services.

Although Fastify is not designed to rely on middleware, it supports the use of Express/Connect-style middleware, mainly for compatibility or integration of specific functions. Middleware can access the request and response objects, execute code, modify the request and response objects, terminate the request processing chain, or call the next middleware in the call stack. When using Fastify middleware, caution is required, as improper use may bypass some optimizations of Fastify and affect performance.

Hooks are a mechanism in Fastify that allows developers to intervene and execute logic at different stages of the request lifecycle (such as after the request is received, before the route is resolved, before the response is sent, etc.). Hooks can be used to execute preprocessing or postprocessing logic, such as permission checking, request logging, modifying responses, etc. Fastify provides various types of hooks (such as onRequest, preHandler, onSend, etc.), allowing developers to finely control different stages of request processing.

These components work together to provide Fastify with great flexibility and scalability while maintaining the high-performance characteristics of the framework.

Components in Fastify (plugins, decorators, middleware, and hooks) follow a certain execution order and priority, which determines their timing of action in the request processing flow. Understanding this execution flow is crucial for designing efficient and reliable Fastify applications:

  1. : Loaded when the application starts and executed in the order of registration. After the application starts, the plugin settings are solidified, and the functions defined in the plugins (such as routes, hooks, decorators) can be used for each subsequent request.
  2. : There is no clear execution time for them. The methods or properties added to the decorated objects (Fastify instance, request, reply) are immediately available and remain valid throughout the life cycle of the objects.
  3. : Executed early in the processing flow of each request, specifically before route matching. Middleware can modify the request and reply objects or decide whether to pass the request to the next processor.
  4. : Follow a specific execution order, which is reflected in the request processing flow:
    • : Executed immediately after the request is received, before any other processing.
    • : Executed before the request body is parsed.
    • : Executed before route-level validation.
    • : Executed before the route processing function.
    • : Executed before the response is serialized, before sending it to the client.
    • : Executed before the response is sent to the client, but after serialization.
    • : Executed after the response is completely sent to the client.
  1. : Do not directly participate in the interruption of request processing but can register hooks or middleware that affect the process.
  2. : Do not control the process, so they do not participate in interruptions.
  3. : Can interrupt the request processing flow. For example, not calling next() or sending a response can stop the subsequent processing.
  4. : Specific hooks (such as preHandler) can decide whether to continue processing the request or directly send a response, thus interrupting the subsequent process.

Understanding the execution order and interruption capabilities of these components is crucial for building Fastify applications that behave as expected.

  1. : A new request enters the system.
  2. : Determine the route corresponding to the request.
  3. : Record the instance logs related to the request.
  4. : Execute the hook function, which can be used for unified request preprocessing.
  5. : Execute the hook function before the request body is parsed.
  6. : Parse the request body.
  7. : Execute the hook function before route validation.
  8. : Perform route validation:
    • If the validation fails→[400]: Return a 400 error response.
    • If the validation passes→↓
  9. : Execute the hook function before the route processing function, and operations such as permission checking can be performed.
  10. : Execute the user-defined route processing function.
  11. : Prepare the response content.
  12. : Execute the hook function before the response is serialized.
  13. : Execute the hook function before the response is sent.
  14. : Send the response.
  15. : Execute the hook function after the response is completely sent.

Fastify's is one of its basic features. The encapsulation context determines which decorators, registered hooks, and plugins can be used for routes. Each context only inherits from the parent context, and the parent context cannot access any entities in its descendant contexts. If the default situation does not meet the requirements, fastify-plugin can be used to break the encapsulation context, making the content registered in the descendant contexts available in the containing parent context.

8. JSON Schema Validation for Routes

JSON Schema is combined with routes in Fastify to validate client request data and format response data. By defining JSON Schema, it can be ensured that the incoming request data meets specific format and type requirements, and at the same time, control the structure of the response data, improving the robustness and security of the API.

  1. is defined, describing the expected format of the request body, including required fields and the types of each field.
  2. In the route configuration, is applied to the request body () through the property, and Fastify will automatically validate whether the incoming request data conforms to the defined schema.
  3. The of the response is defined to ensure that the response data structure meets the expectations.

In this way, it can be ensured that all incoming requests are strictly validated and all responses conform to the预定格式, enhancing the robustness of the API and the predictability for users.

9.1 Hooks Handling Login Status Issues

The following example requires that the and parameters must be passed in a post request:

If you do not define the hook and the custom in the route, when the of a POST request does not conform to the defined JSON Schema, Fastify will, by default, return a 400 error (Bad Request). The response body of this error will contain information about which fields do not meet the requirements, but this information is automatically generated by Fastify and may not be as specific or clear as a custom error message. By default, if only the parameter is passed and the parameter is missing, Fastify will return a response containing error details, similar to:

Finally, I recommend a platform that is most suitable for deploying NodeJS services:

🚀 Build with Your Favorite Language

Develop effortlessly in JavaScript, Python, Go, or Rust.

🌍 Deploy Unlimited Projects for Free

Only pay for what you use—no requests, no charges.

⚡ Pay-as-You-Go, No Hidden Costs

No idle fees, just seamless scalability.

items.title: Point Clicker: How to Maximize Ad Engagement Like Never Before
items.author: Entyx
items.content:
Think clicking is just for fun? Not when it rewards you! Today, we are excited to introduce you to Entyx's latest innovation – a Twitch widget that turns clicks into real traffic & engagement! Drive users to Telegram, websites & apps.

Want more engagement? Need targeted traffic? Looking for measurable campaign results? If you’ve answered “YES” three times, then let us click you through the article!

How Does the Point Clicker Work?

Point Clicker by Entyx is an interactive Twitch widget that boosts engagement by rewarding users for clicks. Let’s unlock the mechanics behind the magic of this interactive widget for Twitch!

  1. Streamers install the widget on Twitch.
  2. Viewers click a branded icon to earn points.
  3. Once a goal is reached (e.g., 100 clicks), users unlock a reward.
  4. Users are redirected via a QR code or referral link to your chosen destination.

We prepared a YouTube video about it, feel free to watch here and join our channel.

Where can you direct users?

    For example, if you want to direct users to any of your pages, you can offer a reward for the target action - clicking the exact amount of times. Reward can cost any sum of money, but if a user reaches 100 clicks, they get one for free. However, to claim it, they need to log in.

    We collect their usernames and track all activity on the Entyx platform, using our comprehensive 360-degree AI-driven dashboards. Obtained user traffic is then redirected to the source you need by means that users receive a QR code directing them to your targeted destination.

    Why Agencies & Advertisers Love Point Clicker

    Here’s why streamers, advertisers, and agencies can’t afford to miss Point Clicker:

    • Boosts audience engagement Viewers stay longer and actively interact with the stream.
    • Seamlessly redirects Twitch users to websites, Telegram, apps, and more.
    • You control the referral links, rewards, and engagement mechanics.
    • Advanced Analytics & Tracking An advertiser or an agency gains full visibility into streamer performance via the Entyx platform, tracking clicks, nicknames, session durations, and conversions to measure campaign success effectively using Entyx’s 360° AI-powered dashboard.

    Unique Interactive Features

    • Streamers and advertisers can customize widget placement on the screen for optimal visibility.
    • Point Clicker is fully adjustable and you can design it in accordance with your brand’s identity: use your brand’s main theme color, add your logo, messaging, change the design of three banners at once, rotate objects and personalize it literally in every single way.
    • Unlike other Twitch widgets, Point Clicker goes beyond entertainment – it motivates real and targeted user actions.
    • An advertiser or an agency gets access to a detailed analytics dashboard on Entyx, providing insights on clicks, traffic sources, conversion rates, and viewer activity.

    Easy Integration in Just 4 Steps

    1. The streamer installs the widget on Twitch via a simple link.
    2. An advertiser or an agency adds streamers to the Entyx platform and tracks their performance.
    3. Once the click target is met, the audience unlocks a reward – whether it’s a link, promo code, or exclusive content.
    4. An advertiser or an agency evaluates campaign success – identifying top-performing streamers and the most effective reward strategies.

    Example: If you’ve partnered with a streamer—let’s say you agreed to buy advertising from them—you can set a condition that the streamer must install the Point Clicker widget to track clicks, engagement, and other interactions.

    1. We provide the streamer with a Twitch widget link.
    2. The streamer clicks the link, installs the widget, and a default image appears on their screen.
    3. The widget description will include an Ad Banner.

    Once installed, the advertiser can see the streamer listed in their personal dashboard (as shown in the screenshot). From there, they can:

    • Edit the streamer’s campaign settings.
    • Choose when the widget should be active (e.g., only during a specific game session).
    • Customize the visuals, replacing the default image with any other banner.

    The widget isn’t limited to just click-based campaigns. Advertisers can use it however they like—their only task is to find a streamer and request them to install the extension.

    Once installed, the system links the streamer’s account with the advertiser's, allowing them to:

    1. Track detailed stats in real-time.
    2. See full engagement analytics inside the widget.
    3. Monitor a global leaderboard showing total points collected.
    4. View individual leaderboards for each streamer.

    So, here’s the core idea:

    You have a group of streamers running your campaign. After purchasing ads from 10 streamers, you can analyze overall performance—for example, how many people clicked the widget and reached the 100-click goal. Let’s say 50 people completed the task—now you can see which streamers delivered the best results and decide who to continue working with.

    From there, you can drill down into individual streamer stats, tracking:

    • The number of users who reached the goal (e.g., 100 clicks).
    • The total number of partial clicks from all users.
    • If users provided their Telegram, it will appear in the list; otherwise, only their Twitch username will be shown.

    Clicks = Audience Retention Every click represents active engagement, meaning viewers are staying longer. While 100 clicks is just an example, you can set any threshold in the campaign editor—even just 1 click to trigger a reward instantly.

    If you set a 100-click goal, but the stats show that 90% of users only reach 40, you can adjust the threshold to 20 clicks to maximize conversions.

    You can set campaign start and end dates in the dashboard. A campaign can run all day (from 12:00 AM to 11:59 PM).

    Even if a streamer starts their stream at 8 PM, the widget will remain active until midnight.

    The campaign will automatically continue on the following day if scheduled.

    This way, businesses can launch and run campaigns seamlessly, ensuring they get maximum exposure and conversions from Twitch audiences.

    To sum it up, Point Clicker is an innovative Twitch engagement tool that turns passive viewers into active participants, driving real traffic and measurable business results. With its flexible customization, seamless integration, and data-driven approach through Entyx, it’s the perfect solution for streamers and advertisers looking to maximize their impact. We will gladly customize Point Clicker and adjust it to your needs. Sign up today to test Point Clicker!

items.title: An Introduction to HLS Content Delivery: What You Need to Know
items.author: Sourav Malhotra
items.content:
In today’s digital world, streaming high-quality video content has become essential. One of the most widely used streaming technologies is HLS content delivery. Whether you're watching live sports, an online class, or your favorite movie, HLS ensures a smooth, adaptive, and scalable streaming experience. But what exactly is HLS, and why is it so popular? Let's dive in.

Understanding HLS (HTTP Live Streaming)

HLS (HTTP Live Streaming) is a protocol developed by Apple that delivers video content over the internet. Unlike traditional streaming, which relies on a single continuous file, HLS breaks videos into smaller segments and delivers them dynamically, adjusting to network conditions in real time.

Key Components of HLS Content Delivery Media Segments

HLS works by dividing a video into short segments, usually a few seconds long. This makes buffering less noticeable and allows for adaptive streaming.

These files act as indexes, guiding the video player on which media segments to load and in what order.

Adaptive Bitrate Streaming

One of HLS's most powerful features is its ability to adjust video quality automatically based on the viewer’s internet speed.

How HLS Differs from Other Streaming Protocols

HLS is often compared to protocols like RTMP, DASH, and MPEG-DASH. While RTMP requires a dedicated server, HLS works over standard HTTP, making it more efficient. Compared to DASH, HLS has broader support, particularly on Apple devices.

Benefits of HLS Content Delivery

Adaptive Streaming for a Better User Experience HLS ensures smooth playback by adjusting video quality dynamically, reducing buffering and interruptions.

  1. Wide Compatibility Across Devices HLS is natively supported on Apple devices, most web browsers, and even Android phones, making it an ideal choice for cross-platform streaming.

2.Scalability for Large Audiences Since HLS uses HTTP-based delivery, it can leverage CDNs (Content Delivery Networks) to handle massive traffic loads efficiently.

3.Security Features of HLS

HLS supports AES-128 encryption and token-based authentication, ensuring secure content delivery and protecting against piracy.

Real-World Applications of HLS Content Delivery

  • Video-on-Demand (VOD) Streaming Platforms like Netflix, Hulu, and Disney+ rely on HLS for seamless on-demand content delivery, ensuring high-quality playback.

  • Live Streaming Events Sports broadcasts, concerts, and webinars use HLS to deliver real-time content with minimal buffering.

  • E-learning and Webinars Educational platforms use HLS to stream high-quality lessons, ensuring accessibility for students on various devices.

How to Implement HLS for Your Content

Choose the right encoding tool – Use software like FFmpeg to convert videos into HLS format.

Create M3U8 playlist files – These files organize media segments for smooth playback.

Optimize segment length – Shorter segments reduce latency, while longer segments improve efficiency.

Challenges and Limitations of HLS

  • Latency Issues and How to Reduce Them HLS has higher latency than RTMP, but Low-Latency HLS (LL-HLS) is improving real-time streaming capabilities.

  • Bandwidth Considerations Higher resolution streaming requires more bandwidth. Adaptive bitrate encoding helps manage bandwidth efficiently.

  • Compatibility with Older Browsers Some older browsers may require additional plugins to support HLS streaming.

Future of HLS Content Delivery

  • Low-Latency HLS (LL-HLS) Apple’s LL-HLS significantly reduces latency, making live streaming more responsive.

  • AI-Powered Content Delivery Optimization AI and machine learning are being integrated into streaming platforms to optimize bandwidth usage and improve user experience.

HLS content delivery has revolutionized video streaming, offering adaptive playback, scalability, and security. As technology evolves, improvements like LL-HLS and AI-driven optimization will continue to shape the future of online video distribution.

  1. What makes HLS content delivery better than traditional streaming methods? HLS uses adaptive bitrate streaming, ensuring a buffer-free experience even with fluctuating internet speeds.

  2. Can HLS be used for both live and on-demand streaming? Yes! HLS is ideal for both live broadcasts and video-on-demand (VOD) streaming.

  3. What are the main challenges of using HLS? Higher latency and bandwidth consumption are common challenges, but LL-HLS is helping reduce latency.

  4. How does HLS improve video quality? By dynamically adjusting video resolution based on network conditions, HLS ensures smooth playback.

  5. What is the future of HLS in the streaming industry? With advancements in low-latency streaming, AI optimization, and enhanced security, HLS will remain a leading content delivery method for years to come.

items.title: How AI-Powered wearables Can Detect Early Signs of Illness
items.author: SEO Glaxit
items.content:
In today’s fast-paced world, staying healthy should be a top priority. However, many people tend to ignore small symptoms that could lead to serious health problems later. Thanks to technology, we might not need to wait for a doctor’s appointment anymore. AI-powered wearables are changing the way we track and understand our health. These smart devices can detect early signs of illness, often before we even feel sick. This not only helps in early diagnosis but also saves lives.

What Are AI-Powered Wearables?

AI-powered wearables are smart devices like fitness bands, smart watches, or even patches that monitor your body’s activities. They collect data such as heart rate, sleep patterns, oxygen levels, temperature, and movement. Artificial Intelligence (AI) analyzes this data to find patterns and detect anything unusual. These devices are becoming more popular every day. According to a report by Statista, over 1.1 billion wearable devices were in use globally by 2022, and the number keeps increasing. This proves that people are trusting these gadgets to help them live healthier lives.

How Do These Wearables Work?

Let’s break it down. Suppose you’re wearing a smart watch. It tracks your heart rate, your oxygen level, and even how well you sleep. Now, if your heart rate suddenly becomes irregular or your temperature goes up, the AI flags it as a warning. The system might even send an alert to your phone or doctor. In some cases, it can recommend that you get a health check-up.

Benefits of Detecting Illness Early

Detecting illness early makes a world of difference. It can save lives, reduce treatment costs, and help people recover faster. Here are some major benefits:

Timely Medical Attention: If the wearable notices an issue, you may be able to visit the doctor before symptoms get worse. Personalized Health Data: These gadgets give you insights about your body. You don’t need to rely on guesswork.

Peace of Mind: You can feel more relaxed knowing that something is keeping an eye on your health.

Let’s say Maria, a 35-year-old teacher, wears a smart ring that tracks her pulse and sleep. One day, the device detects that her heart rate is higher than usual while she sleeps. If she ignores it, she might face heart issues later. But because the device warns her, she goes to the doctor early and finds out she has a minor heart problem that could have turned serious.

Many companies are already using AI in wearable's. Apple Watches can alert users about irregular heart rhythms. Fit bit devices track sleep and stress levels. Our Rings monitor temperature changes, which can help predict illness.

Moreover, hospitals and clinics are starting to use wearable technology too. For instance, if a patient is being monitored at home, the wearable can send real-time data to the doctor. This means fewer hospital visits, more comfort, and quicker responses. If hospitals integrated these wearables into their systems, they could improve patient care drastically.

The Role of AI in Making Predictions

AI doesn’t just collect data, it thinks. It looks at the data over time and finds patterns. For instance, if your blood pressure rises slowly over a few weeks, AI will notice it even if you don’t. If you’re under stress, your sleep pattern may change, and AI can connect the dots.

Think of it like this: AI is like a friend who pays close attention and remembers everything. If something’s off, it speaks up. That’s how it helps in predicting illnesses even when symptoms are not very clear.

Challenges and Considerations

Despite all the benefits, AI-powered wearables also come with some challenges. Privacy is a major concern. These devices collect sensitive data, and users must be careful about who has access to it. Also, not every device is 100% accurate. If the AI makes a wrong prediction, it could cause panic. Additionally, some people might not feel comfortable relying too much on machines for health. But if used correctly, these devices should be seen as helpful tools rather than replacements for doctors.

What If Everyone Used AI-powered Wearables?

If everyone had access to these smart devices, health monitoring would become much easier. Governments and health systems could track the spread of illnesses like any chronic disease in real time. If someone’s wearable detected a symptom, they could isolate quickly and prevent the illness from spreading. Had more people worn wearables during the early days of the COVID-19 pandemic, it might have helped in detecting outbreaks sooner. This shows how powerful these tools can be when used widely.

The Future of Health Monitoring

In the future, AI-powered wearables will likely become smarter, smaller, and more affordable. We might see clothes with sensors or even smart tattoos. These tools could change the way we think about health forever. If these innovations are supported by strong data privacy laws and user-friendly designs, they will truly become everyday health partners for millions of people.

Glaxit: Driving AI Innovation in Health Technology

Companies like Glaxit are already playing a major role in the digital transformation of healthcare. Glaxit is a forward-thinking software agency that develops intelligent solutions using AI and modern technology. If your business wants to explore smart wearable integrations or healthcare tech solutions, Glaxit can provide expert services. With a team that understands both technology and user behavior, Glaxit is ready to help you build a healthier future for apps & websites.

items.title: Go's fmt Package
items.author: Malika7188
items.content:
The fmt package in Go (Golang) is one of the most commonly used packages, providing formatted I/O functionalities. It allows developers to format strings, print to the console, and read user inputs.

Introduction to the fmt Package

The fmt package is part of Go's standard library and provides functionalities for formatting and printing data. The package's name, fmt, stands for "format." It is extensively used for outputting data to the console and reading user input.

To use the fmt package, simply import it:

These are the most basic and frequently used functions; Print(), Println(), and Printf().

The Print() function outputs data to the console without adding a newline at the end.

package main

import "fmt"

func main() {
    fmt.Print("Hello, ")
    fmt.Print("World!")
}

This function prints each argument separated by a space and appends a newline at the end.

package main
import "fmt"
func main() {
    fmt.Println("Hello, World!")
    fmt.Println("Go is awesome!")
}


Hello, World!
Go is awesome!

The Printf() function is used for formatted strings. It requires a format specifier, which defines how the output should be formatted.

package main
import "fmt"
func main() {
    name := "Alice"
    age := 30
    fmt.Printf("Name: %s, Age: %d\n", name, age)
}

The power of fmt lies in its format specifiers, also known as verbs. These verbs define how different types of data are printed.

Floating point (default precision)
Floating point (2 decimal places)fmt.Printf("%.2f", 3.1415)
fmt.Printf("%#v", []int{1,2})
fmt.Printf("%+v", struct{Name string}{Name: "Alice"})

You can control the width and precision of numbers using format verbs:

package main

import "fmt"

func main() {
    pi := 3.1415926535
    fmt.Printf("Default: %f\n", pi)
    fmt.Printf("Precision: %.2f\n", pi)
    fmt.Printf("Width: %8.2f\n", pi)
}

Default: 3.141593
Precision: 3.14
Width:     3.14

You can adjust the width and alignment of strings:

package main

import "fmt"

func main() {
    fmt.Printf("Left align: %-10s!\n", "Go")
    fmt.Printf("Right align: %10s!\n", "Go")
}

Left align: Go        !
Right align:         Go!

4. Reading Input with fmt

The fmt package also provides functions to read user input from the console. Scan() reads input from standard input (stdin). It stops reading when it encounters a space, newline, or EOF.

package main

import "fmt"

func main() {
    var name string
    fmt.Print("Enter your name: ")
    fmt.Scan(&name)
    fmt.Println("Hello,", name)
}

Scanf() allows formatted input, similar to Printf().

package main

import "fmt"

func main() {
    var name string
    var age int
    fmt.Print("Enter your name and age: ")
    fmt.Scanf("%s %d", &name, &age)
    fmt.Printf("Name: %s, Age: %d\n", name, age)
}

Scanln() is similar to Scan(), but it stops reading at a newline.

package main

import "fmt"

func main() {
    var input string
    fmt.Print("Enter some text: ")
    fmt.Scanln(&input)
    fmt.Println("You entered:", input)
}

5. Working with Error Handling

The fmt package provides the Errorf() function to create formatted error messages.

This function returns an error that formats according to a format specifier.

package main

import (
    "errors"
    "fmt"
)

func divide(a, b int) (int, error) {
    if b == 0 {
        return 0, fmt.Errorf("cannot divide by zero")
    }
    return a / b, nil
}

func main() {
    result, err := divide(4, 0)
    if err != nil {
        fmt.Println("Error:", err)
    } else {
        fmt.Println("Result:", result)
    }
}

Error: cannot divide by zero

6. Best Practices and Tips

  • Use Printf() for structured output: When you need control over the format, Printf() is your go-to function.
  • Always handle errors: When using functions like Scanf() and Scanln(), check for errors to avoid unexpected behaviors.
  • Leverage formatting verbs: Understanding the full range of formatting verbs can help you achieve the desired output and avoid manual string manipulations.

The fmt package is a fundamental part of Go's standard library that simplifies formatted I/O operations. By mastering fmt, you can handle string formatting, console printing, and user input efficiently, making your Go programs more robust and user-friendly.

items.title: Idea Code App: My Build Story
items.author: Abdul Rafay
items.content:
Alright, let's hear it. We've all been there—you have an idea for an app that could help you out, and you realize it could help others as well. But you don’t know how to create a good application, or moreover, you have no idea how to build one.

Now, you go on YouTube and start searching, and the number of videos is overwhelming. You watch one, then two, but when it comes down to actually starting from nothing, things start to get a little rough.

You don’t know the app flow, how users will interact with it, or even what features you need. And that’s perfectly normal. As a beginner trying to build an app, you don’t yet understand how things should flow.

The purpose of this blog post is to show you all how to build an app from scratch. I’ll go through everything—from the idea to development, all the bugs I encountered, how to solve them, and how AI can be helpful.

For this app, I was writing my notes on a website and could access them via the web. But what about mobile? It was really inconvenient to open a browser, enter a URL, and then visit my notes. And sometimes, the internet isn’t reliable. So, now what?

The solution for me was simple—I needed an app where I could read all my lecture notes with offline access.

With that in mind, I started writing down what I needed. The core idea was that I should be able to read my notes without an internet connection. My website already has API access, so I could call that API and render the content in the app, right?

It’s simple and can be done.

Once the core idea was set, it was time to think about what features would support it and ensure the app wasn’t misused. So, I added these features:

    These features would make note reading much smoother and more convenient.

    Once you have all the features listed and know what you want, the next step is choosing a good tech stack. People say it doesn’t matter what tech stack you choose, but let me tell you—when it comes to apps, you generally have four options for the frontend:

      And for the backend, there are several good options:

        All of these options have their own pros and cons. If you’d like to see a core difference between Firebase and Supabase, check out my previous blog post.

        I haven’t used PocketBase or Appwrite yet, but I’ve heard they are good options.

        For me, I’ll be using Flutter for the UI and Appwrite for the backend. The backend will handle user sync, note-taking, user authentication, and the auth gate—nothing more than that. The UI will be coded using Flutter because I use Flutter for everything, and I love its flexibility.

        Don’t choose blindly—do your research and pick what makes you comfortable and what you feel confident coding in. If I weren’t used to Flutter, I would have gone with React Native, since I know how React works but have no experience with React Native. So, choose what works best for you.

        You know the features, you know how to choose a tech stack, now it's time to define the flow. By flow, I mean how the user will navigate from one page to another. And this is actually very simple. When a user installs your application, what will they see first? It could be a Login Screen, an Introduction Screen, a Splash Screen, anything—it all depends on your thought process and your application.

        Here’s the flow I created for my application:

        The auth flow shows how the authentication process is handled in the app.

        • Installed -> Introduction Screen -> Login -> Success -> Home Screen
        • Installed -> Introduction Screen -> Login -> Failed -> Error Message
        • Installed -> Introduction Screen -> Register -> User Created -> Login -> Success -> Home Screen
        • Installed -> Introduction Screen -> Register -> User NOT Created -> Error Message
        • User Logged In -> Home Screen
        • User Not Logged In -> Login Screen

        This flow illustrates how the app operates under the hood.

        • App Opened -> Internet Connection -> Call API (Get Data) -> Store in Local Database -> Show to User
        • App Opened -> NO Internet Connection -> Switch to Offline Mode
        • Open App -> Switch to Note-Taking UI -> Create Note -> Write Some Content -> Save Content

        You can see how the app flow is structured. This is just the beginning. As you add more features, you’ll naturally have more flows to define. It’s better to set up a structure early and start coding, because as you develop, the flow will reveal itself more clearly.

        I’m only able to describe this flow because I’ve actually done it—I’ve built the app, faced real challenges, and figured out how each UI should connect to the next.

        Okay, you have everything set up. Now what? This is the question most people don’t know how to answer. You’ve got the design, the flow, the idea, and the tech stack. What’s next?

        Here’s the short answer: Google and use AI.

        In the beginning, Google . Don’t just rely on AI—you’ll forget what you learn that way. Google, Stack Overflow, documentation—they'll stick in your brain better. Watch some tutorials to get a solid foundation. And then... start coding.

        Now, let me be honest. It wasn’t all sunshine and rainbows.

        I initially aimed to finish the app in a week. If you look at my GitHub, it did take me about a week—but, oh boy, it wasn’t easy.

        Everything started fine. I created the UI, set up Appwrite as the backend, and things were rolling smoothly. Until... Appwrite refused to add users to my database. I tried everything, but the issues just kept stacking up. So, I decided: forget this, I’m switching to Firebase.

        Thankfully, I had separated my UI from my backend. All I had to do was change the backend implementation. Nothing broke in the frontend—lesson learned: keep frontend and backend loosely coupled via a repository file where all the logic meets.

        API Struggles & Hive Mastery

        Once Firebase was up and running, it was time to fetch notes from my website's API. I used the package from pub.dev, which worked fine. But for some reason, the body of the notes wasn’t coming through.

        To handle offline data, I integrated Hive, which was great, but initially tricky to set up. I created models, commands, and started syncing API data to Hive. The issue? Still no note body! Eventually, after hours of frustration, it turned out the API itself had the problem, not Hive.

        Once fixed, data was flowing and saving properly.

        UI Layer & Card-Based Madness

        Next challenge: UI design. There were multiple subjects, multiple lectures, and I wanted a card-based interface. One card leads to another, leading to lecture notes at the end. I made templates to dynamically pass data—whether it's research notes, lecture notes, etc.—and the experience became seamless.

        Search: Fizzy Finder or Bust

        Search was another beast. Firebase’s built-in search? Nah, it won’t cut it. I wanted powerful search capabilities, so I turned to Fizzy Finder and built my own search functionality. This was crucial for scaling.

        After deploying V1, I decided to add a note-taking module. The big question: What editor? Markdown, Rich Text, something else? I found Flutter Quill—perfect!

        I installed it, ran the emulator... and everything broke. Dependency hell. Turns out Flutter Quill v2.9 had conflicts with my Introduction Screen package. I had to pick one. So, Introduction Screen had to go. I rebuilt the entire introduction UI from scratch with custom animations and flows. Painful—but fun.

        Once that was sorted, Hive became my best friend again. I created and services. I set up controllers, wired everything up, and data was being saved smoothly.

        For displaying notes, I wanted something like Google Keep. I wasn’t sure how to achieve this, so I turned to Google Studio AI—it generated a UI I didn’t , but hey, it worked.

        CRUD’s Biggest Nightmare: Deleting Notes

        Deleting notes became my nemesis. I wanted a recycle bin feature. Hive doesn’t support moving content between boxes. So, I had to manually copy content from one Hive box to another, then delete it, and vice versa if restoring. Hive team, if you’re reading: please add move support—it would make life so much easier!

        One thing left: the sync layer.

        All notes were stored locally, but users needed backups in the cloud. Easy in theory, painful in practice.

        Where should sync happen? How should users know if their data is backed up? At first, I triggered sync on save, update, delete—but it slowed down the app by 2-5 seconds. Not acceptable.

        Solution? Sync runs when the app launches. It detects changes and pushes them to Firebase. I added settings to give users control over sync, and made sure sync runs off the main thread so the UI stays smooth. (Shoutout to AI for helping me figure this out—I was lost!)

        After all the battles, late nights, and debug sessions, I finally had a polished app. I called it .

        Every flow, feature, and issue taught me something valuable. And trust me, whatever app you’re building, you’ll hit similar hurdles. Just stay patient, keep separating concerns, Google like crazy, and enjoy the ride!

        The app isn’t live on the Google Play Store just yet, but you can still give it a spin! Simply head over to my website or download it directly using the link below:

        Curious to see how I built this? Check out the entire codebase on GitHub:

        I’d love to hear your thoughts! Tell me how I coded, what you liked (or didn’t), and how badly I might have messed things up 😄.

        Drop your feedback, suggestions, or even a roast in the comment section—I’m all ears! 👇

        Now, it’s time for some final thoughts. I did this not just because I’m getting a blog post and a future video out of it, but more importantly—because it was fun. Coding for fun is something I’ve missed, and writing down my thoughts, sharing my experiences—that’s something I truly enjoy.

        Here’s the thing: don’t be afraid. You’re an engineer. You’re to break things, to see how they unfold, to figure out how things can be done. Coding is fun. Find an idea, and start building. It might look like chaos at times—some parts definitely felt like that—but I genuinely enjoyed every bit of it.

        Until then, goodbye nerds.

        Check out the original article at rafay99.com for the full story!

items.title: How Can I migrate from Drupal 7 to Backdrop CMS?
items.author: Jaswinder Singh
items.content:
Drupal 7’s end-of-life (Jan 2025) has left many site owners looking for alternatives. One great option is Backdrop CMS, a Drupal 7 fork designed for small to mid-sized websites. It keeps Drupal 7’s best features while improving performance and ease of use.

If you’re considering migrating from Drupal 7 to Backdrop CMS, here’s a step-by-step guide to ensure a smooth transition.

  • Same Familiar Structure: Built on Drupal 7’s architecture.
  • Lightweight & Faster: Improved performance with a smaller footprint.
  • Lower Maintenance Costs: No complex updates like Drupal 9/10.

Step 1: Backup Your Drupal 7 Site

Before migrating, make sure to backup your database, files, and themes to avoid data loss.

Step 2: Set Up a Backdrop CMS Environment

  • Download and install Backdrop CMS on a staging server.
  • Ensure PHP 7.3+ is installed.

*Step 3: Migrate Data from Drupal 7 *

  • Backdrop CMS has a built-in upgrade path:
  • Place your Drupal 7 database in your Backdrop site directory.
  • Modify settings.php to use the Drupal 7 database.
  • Run the Backdrop upgrade script (upgrade.php).
  • The system will migrate nodes, users, taxonomy, and menu items automatically.

Step 4: Rebuild Your Site

  • Backdrop CMS has a Layout system instead of Drupal 7’s block system, so you may need to:
  • Reconfigure Views (Backdrop has Views built-in).
  • Adjust Custom Modules (Some D7 modules are now part of the Backdrop core).

  • Test Content & Permissions to ensure everything works as expected. **

  • Check for missing features.

  • Enable caching for better performance.

  • Once satisfied, switch your DNS settings and make your Backdrop site live.

Backdrop CMS is an excellent choice if you want a Drupal 7-like experience without complex migrations. It’s a cost-effective, long-term solution for small businesses, nonprofits, and organizations that don’t want to rebuild their sites entirely on Drupal 9/10.

items.title: C4.5 Decision Trees in Go: Optimizing for Performance and Scalability
items.author: Bernad Okumu
items.content:
Decision trees are a fundamental machine learning algorithm used for classification and regression tasks. Among them, , an improvement over , is widely known for handling both categorical and continuous data, pruning trees to reduce overfitting, and dealing with missing values. Implementing C4.5 in presents unique challenges and opportunities, particularly in optimizing for performance and scalability.

Why Implement C4.5 in Go?

Go is well-suited for high-performance computing due to its:

  • : Go compiles directly to machine code, avoiding runtime interpretation overhead.
  • : Goroutines and channels allow parallel execution, crucial for large datasets.
  • : A balance between garbage collection and low-level memory handling.
  • : Go’s lightweight nature makes it ideal for handling large datasets efficiently.

Core Concepts of C4.5 Algorithm

C4.5 builds a decision tree using and as key metrics for splitting nodes. The key steps include:

  1. : Measure impurity in a dataset.
  2. Compute Information Gain Ratio: Evaluate the usefulness of a feature.
  3. : Select the feature with the highest gain ratio.
  4. : Convert numerical data into discrete bins.
  5. : Reduce overfitting by removing irrelevant branches.

1. Efficient Data Structures

Using instead of traditional object-oriented classes keeps memory footprint low.

2. Parallelizing Training with Goroutines

Instead of iterating through all possible splits sequentially, we can use goroutines for parallel computation.

By spawning multiple goroutines, we improve efficiency when working with large datasets.

Go’s garbage collector is efficient, but large datasets can cause overhead. Using helps reuse memory and reduce allocations.

4. Efficient Sorting for Continuous Values

Sorting is required for handling continuous data splits. Instead of a naive sort, Go’s function is optimized for performance.

Visualization: Understanding the Decision Tree Structure

To better visualize the structure of a trained C4.5 tree, here’s an example of a simple decision tree graph:

        [Feature1 < 5.0]
       /              \
  [Feature2 < 3.5]    [Class: Yes]
  /          \
[Class: No]   [Feature3 < 2.0]
              /           \
        [Class: No]     [Class: Yes]

For performance visualization, tools like in Go can be used to analyze memory and CPU usage.

 go  cpu.prof
 go tool pprof cpu.prof

Scaling Up: Handling Large Datasets

  • : Instead of loading all data into memory, stream data in chunks.
  • : Integrate with frameworks like (via Go bindings) or for parallel computation across multiple nodes.
  • : Store intermediate results in SQLite or a key-value store for checkpointing.

Implementing C4.5 Decision Trees in Go is a powerful approach for creating scalable and efficient classification models. By leveraging Go’s concurrency, memory management, and efficient data structures, we can significantly optimize performance.

items.title: How to Get Daily GitHub Follower Updates via Email (No Coding Needed!)
items.author: Sumonta Saha Mridul
items.content:
Are you tired of manually checking your GitHub profile to see who followed you? What if you could get daily email notifications with your latest GitHub followers—without writing a single line of code?

In this step-by-step guide, I’ll show you how to set up an automated GitHub Follower Notification Bot that sends you email updates. Whether you're a developer, open-source contributor, or just curious about your GitHub growth, this tool will save you time and keep you informed.

🚀 Why You Need GitHub Follower Email Notifications

  1. Stay updated without logging into GitHub daily.
  2. Track your growth and engagement effortlessly.
  3. No coding skills required—just follow simple steps.
  4. Fully automated—set it once and forget it!

Before we begin, make sure you have:

  • ✅ A Gmail account (for email notifications)
  • ✅ 5 minutes to set everything up

📥 Step 1: Fork the GitHub Follower Notification Bot

  • Go to the GitHub Follower Notification Bot repository.
  • Click "Fork" (top-right corner) to create your own copy.

⚙️ Step 2: Configure Your GitHub Token

To let the bot access your follower data, you need a GitHub Personal Access Token (PAT).

  1. Go to GitHub Settings > Developer Settings > Personal Access Tokens.
  2. Click "Generate new token".
  3. Give it a name (e.g., Follower-Bot).
  4. Select the repo and user scopes.
  5. Click "Generate token" and copy it (you won’t see it again!).

⚠️ Keep this token private! Never share it publicly.

📧 Step 3: Set Up Email Notifications

The bot uses Gmail to send notifications. Here’s how to configure it:

  • Enable "Less Secure Apps" in Gmail (or use an App Password if 2FA is enabled).
  • Go to Google Account Security.
  • Turn on "Allow less secure apps" (or generate an App Password).
  • Add Secrets to Your Repository
  • Go to your forked repo → Settings → Secrets → Actions.
Secret Name Value
GITHUB_TOKEN    Your GitHub PAT (from Step 2)
GMAIL_USERNAME  Your Gmail (e.g., you@gmail.com)
GMAIL_PASSWORD  Your Gmail password/App Password
RECIPIENT_EMAIL Email where you want updates

🔄 Step 4: Run the Bot Daily (Automatically!)

The bot uses GitHub Actions to check for new followers every day.

  • Go to the "Actions" tab in your repo.
  • Click "Enable Workflows".

The bot will now run daily at 12:00 AM UTC.

🎉 Done! You’ll now get email updates whenever someone follows you on GitHub.

❓ Why am I not getting emails?

  • Ensure GitHub Actions ran successfully (check the logs).

❓ Can I change the notification frequency?

  • Yes! Edit the .github/workflows/main.yml file and adjust the cron schedule.
  • Yes! Your token and email credentials are stored securely in GitHub Secrets.

Now you can track your GitHub followers effortlessly with zero manual work. This bot is perfect for:

  • Developers who want to monitor their GitHub growth.
  • Open-source maintainers tracking community engagement.
  • Anyone who loves automation!

By following this guide, you’ll never miss a GitHub follower again! 🚀 Let me know how it works for you. Happy coding! 😊

items.title: How to Build a Marketplace — Detailed Guide for Founders
items.author: Linh Ho
items.content:
Since the advent of the internet, websites have undergone significant evolution. Initially, they were seen as virtual homes — digital representations of businesses where customers could learn more about them. Today, websites have become direct sales platforms, enabling businesses to sell products online. With the rise of global trade and diverse business models, we now have countless types of website structures tailored to specific business needs. One such structure is the marketplace.

Unlike traditional business websites where a single company interacts with customers, a marketplace is essentially an online bazaar. It brings together multiple sellers and buyers, enabling transactions on a 24/7 e-commerce platform. This model has gained immense popularity in the era of Industry 4.0 and the sharing economy. Marketplaces offer standout advantages, such as transparent pricing, integrated payment systems, and optimized delivery times. These features not only help businesses reach a larger customer base but also significantly boost revenue and profitability.

However, building a successful and unique marketplace requires careful planning and execution. I cut the guide from . Let’s check!

A Step-by-Step Guide for Founders to build an Online Marketplace

Step 1: Choosing the Right Type of Marketplace No matter what products or services you’re selling, the first step on how to build a marketplace is identifying the right business model.

Marketplaces can be categorized in two ways: ‍Classification Based on Business Partners

  • C2C Marketplace (Consumer-to-Consumer): This model connects individual sellers or small businesses with consumers on platforms like Shopify or Facebook Marketplace. It’s ideal for individuals with limited capital or resources, as it allows them to start selling with minimal barriers.
  • B2C Marketplace (Business-to-Consumer): This model connects products from businesses or authorized distributors directly to consumers. Unlike C2C, B2C allows businesses to operate as official stores (e.g., Shopee or Lazada Mall), ensuring higher credibility and trust.
  • B2B Marketplace (Business-to-Business): B2B marketplaces facilitate transactions between businesses. Examples include Alibaba.com, which connects manufacturers with wholesalers, and ThomasNet, a platform for industrial suppliers. These platforms often focus on bulk orders, long-term contracts, and specialized industries like manufacturing or construction.

Classification Based on Products

Marketplaces can also be categorized by the types of products they offer:

  • Vertical Marketplace: These platforms focus on a single category of products or services from multiple suppliers. For example, Etsy specializes in handmade and vintage items, while Zillow focuses exclusively on real estate.
  • Horizontal Marketplace: These platforms offer products or services within the same industry or category. A prime example is Traveloka, which provides travel-related services like flight tickets, hotel bookings, and car rentals.
  • Hybrid Marketplace: Hybrid marketplaces offer a wide range of products across multiple categories. Examples include Amazon, which sell everything from electronics and fashion to home goods and office supplies.

Niche marketplaces are gaining traction because they cater to specific audiences with tailored solutions. For instance, Faire (a wholesale marketplace for independent retailers) and Depop (a platform for vintage and unique fashion) have successfully carved out their spaces by addressing the unique needs of their target markets. These platforms often outperform generic ones by offering specialized features, curated experiences, and stronger community engagement.

Step 2: Developing a Product Strategy by Defining Core Features & MVP Development for your marketplace

Step 3: Building a Product Roadmap‍

Step 4: Choosing the Right Tech Stack for Marketplace Development

Time to market is critical for new product development, and your ability to rapidly prototype and develop products ensures viable solutions. Github integration with software development tools makes it easy to seamlessly connect your entire software delivery lifecycle.

Bugs and feature modification requests are inevitable in the development of an online marketplace. However, maintaining a structured tracking system and efficient issue management ensures your team stays informed, organized, and on track. Testing involves both internal quality assurance (QA) teams and real users through alpha, beta, or user acceptance testing, helping to identify and resolve issues before full deployment.

Step 7: Launching & Scaling Your Marketplace with Marketing

Once your marketplace is ready, follow these steps to ensure a successful launch and growth:

  • Soft Launch Strategy: How to test your marketplace before going live
  • ‍A soft launch involves releasing your marketplace to a limited group of users for testing and feedback. This helps you identify and fix any bugs or issues before the official launch, ensuring a smoother experience for your users.
  • User Acquisition: SEO, paid ads, and influencer partnerships for user acquisition
  • A comprehensive marketing strategy is crucial for attracting users to your marketplace. This includes search engine optimization (SEO), paid advertising campaigns, and collaborations with influencers in your niche.
  • Key Metrics to Track: Monitor metrics like Gross Merchandise Value (GMV), customer acquisition cost (CAC), and user retention to measure success.

items.title: Sora vs Veo 2: Which is Better?
items.author: 安萨
items.content:
The advent of artificial intelligence (AI) has revolutionized various industries, with video production being no exception. AI-powered video generators have emerged, enabling creators to produce high-quality videos with unprecedented ease and efficiency. Among the leading tools in this domain are OpenAI's Sora and Google's Veo 2. Both platforms offer innovative features, but a critical question remains: which one produces more realistic videos? This article delves into the capabilities of Sora and Veo 2, comparing their features, performance, and suitability for different applications.

Overview of Sora and Veo 2

Sora is an AI video generation model developed by OpenAI. It enables users to create videos from text prompts, facilitating the production of cinematic-grade videos with a focus on creative expression. Sora is designed to be user-friendly and accessible to a broad range of creators, from beginners to professionals.

Veo 2 is Google's latest AI video generation tool, developed by DeepMind. It specializes in producing high-resolution, realistic videos by accurately simulating physics and motion dynamics. Veo 2 is tailored for professionals who require precise and detailed video outputs.

Video Quality and Resolution

Veo 2 offers ultra-realistic videos with resolutions up to 4K, emphasizing physics accuracy and motion dynamics. This high resolution ensures detailed and lifelike visuals, making it suitable for professional-grade projects.

Sora, while capable of producing cinematic-grade videos, focuses more on creative expression and artistic flair. It supports video generation up to 1080p resolution, which is sufficient for many applications but may not match the detail provided by Veo 2's 4K output.

Realism and Physics Simulation

Veo 2 excels in simulating realistic object motion, physics, and natural interactions. Its advanced algorithms accurately capture the nuances of real-world physics, resulting in videos that closely mimic actual events. For instance, Veo 2 can precisely depict water ripples and reflections, enhancing the overall realism of the video.

Sora, while offering impressive motion quality, places less emphasis on perfect physics simulation. It prioritizes creative storytelling and may incorporate more stylized or imaginative elements, which can sometimes compromise strict realism.

Creative Expression and Flexibility

Sora is designed to unleash imagination and creativity, making it ideal for storytelling, entertainment, and art-focused projects. Its flexibility allows users to generate diverse and imaginative content, catering to a wide range of creative needs.

Veo 2, while capable of creative applications, is primarily focused on delivering realistic and practical scenarios. It is best suited for applications where accuracy and detail are paramount, such as advertising, documentaries, and industry-specific visualizations.

Performance and User Experience

Generation Speed and Video Length

Veo 2 can generate videos up to two minutes in length, with a generation time of approximately 10 minutes per video. This makes it suitable for longer, more detailed projects where quality is prioritized over speed. However, in the current VideoFX preview, the output is limited to eight seconds.

Sora supports video generation up to 20 seconds, with a faster generation time of around 5 minutes per video. This quick turnaround is advantageous for creators who need shorter videos promptly, such as for social media content.

Ease of Use and Accessibility

Sora is designed to be user-friendly and accessible, appealing to both casual users and creative experts. Its intuitive interface and integration with ChatGPT subscriptions make it a convenient tool for a broad audience.

Veo 2, while offering advanced capabilities, is aimed at professionals seeking precision and high-resolution outputs. Its focus on detailed realism may require a steeper learning curve and is best suited for users with specific, high-end video production needs.

Pricing and Commercial Viability

Veo2: Google has not yet announced pricing details.Currently accessible via a waitlist for the VideoFX platform in Google Labs, indicating limited availability but expanding.

Sora offers a subscription-based model, with options such as $20/month or $200/month, providing a more affordable entry point for creators who may not require the ultra-high resolution that Veo 2 offers.

  • Professional Filmmaking and Advertising: Its high-resolution and realistic outputs make it suitable for commercials, documentaries, and high-end film projects.
  • Educational Content and Simulations: The accurate physics simulation is beneficial for creating educational videos and realistic simulations.
  • Fields such as engineering and architecture can leverage Veo 2 for detailed visualizations and presentations.
  • Social Media Content and Short Videos:With its faster generation times and flexibility, Sora is perfect for creating engaging short videos for platforms like Instagram, TikTok, and YouTube. Its cinematic effects are ideal for creators who need quick yet visually appealing content.
  • Creative Storytelling and Art Projects: Sora’s emphasis on creative flexibility makes it a fantastic tool for artists and storytellers looking to produce imaginative, high-quality video narratives. The platform allows for more experimental video generation, enabling creators to push the boundaries of video production.
  • Marketing and Entertainment: Marketing teams and entertainment professionals can leverage Sora’s artistic capabilities to create engaging promotional videos, teasers, and animated content, making it a versatile tool in the marketing space.

For Developers: API Access

CometAPI offer a price far lower than the official price to help you integrate Sora API(model name: sora-1.0-turbo;) and Veo 2 API, and you will get $1 in your account after registering and logging in! Welcome to register and experience CometAPI.

CometAPI acts as a centralized hub for APIs of several leading AI models, eliminating the need to engage with multiple API providers separately.

The choice between Sora and Veo 2 ultimately depends on the creator’s specific needs. If you’re looking for cutting-edge realism, especially for high-end professional projects such as filmmaking, advertising, or scientific simulations, Veo 2 is the clear winner. Its advanced physics simulations, high-resolution outputs, and precise control over environmental factors make it the top choice for creating videos that need to reflect the world as accurately as possible.

However, if you’re a creator or marketer looking for a tool that allows for greater flexibility and creativity in video generation, Sora may be the better option. It excels in producing engaging, stylized videos quickly and efficiently, making it ideal for social media content, creative projects, and short-form videos. Its emphasis on artistic expression makes it an excellent tool for creators who are less concerned with strict realism and more focused on delivering visually compelling stories.

In short, for realism, Veo 2 is your go-to, while for creativity and artistic exploration, Sora is a powerful and versatile tool. Ultimately, your choice will depend on the project you're working on and the level of realism required.

items.title: How to Use LocalStorage, SessionStorage and Cookies in JavaScript
items.author: MAURICE OMBEWA
items.content:
Web storage is a powerful feature in modern browsers that allows you to store data directly on a user's computer. In JavaScript, the three primary ways to handle client-side storage are LocalStorage, SessionStorage and Cookies. In this article, we'll explore each method, explain their differences and provide simple examples to get you started.

is designed for storing data with no expiration date. This means that the data you store remains available even after the browser is closed and reopened. It’s perfect for settings, user preferences, or any other information you’d like to persist across sessions.:

  • : Data persists until explicitly removed.
  • : Approximately 5-10 MB per origin (depending on the browser).
  • : Accessible only within the domain that stored it.
// Save data to localStorage
localStorage.setItem('username', 'Alice');

// Retrieve data from localStorage
const username = localStorage.getItem('username');
console.log(username); // Outputs: Alice

// Remove a specific item
localStorage.removeItem('username');

    // Remove all data from localStorage
    localStorage.clear();

is similar to LocalStorage, but with one key difference: data stored in SessionStorage is cleared when the page session ends. A page session lasts as long as the browser is open, and survives over page reloads and restores. However, once the browser is closed, the data is lost.:

  • : Data is available only during the page session.
  • : Accessible only within the tab or window where it was stored.
  • : Ideal for temporary data, such as shopping cart contents that don’t need to persist after the session.
// Save data to sessionStorage
sessionStorage.setItem('sessionData', 'This is temporary data');

// Retrieve data from sessionStorage
const data = sessionStorage.getItem('sessionData');
console.log(data); // Outputs: This is temporary data

// Remove a specific item
sessionStorage.removeItem('sessionData');

    // Remove all data from sessionStorage
    sessionStorage.clear();

have been around much longer than the Web Storage APIs and are primarily used for storing small pieces of data that need to be sent back to the server with each HTTP request. They can have an expiration date, and you can set options like path, domain, and security flags.:

  • : Typically limited to around 4 KB per cookie.
  • : Automatically sent with every HTTP request to the same domain.
  • : Can be set to expire at a specific time.

Unlike and , do not have a simple API for get/set operations in JavaScript. Instead, you manipulate the document.cookie string.

// Set a cookie that expires in 7 days
const setCookie = (name, value, days) => {
  const date = new Date();
  date.setTime(date.getTime() + days * 24 * 60 * 60 * 1000);
  const expires = "expires=" + date.toUTCString();
  document.cookie = name + "=" + value + ";" + expires + ";path=/";
};

setCookie('user', 'Bob', 7);

// Retrieve a cookie value by name
const getCookie = (name) => {
  const cname = name + "=";
  const decodedCookie = decodeURIComponent(document.cookie);
  const cookieArray = decodedCookie.split(';');
  for (let cookie of cookieArray) {
    cookie = cookie.trim();
    if (cookie.indexOf(cname) === 0) {
      return cookie.substring(cname.length, cookie.length);
    }
  }
  return "";
};

console.log(getCookie('user')); // Outputs: Bob

    // To delete a cookie, set its expiry date to a past date
    const deleteCookie = (name) => {
      document.cookie = name + "=; expires=Thu, 01 Jan 1970 00:00:00 UTC; path=/;";
    };

    deleteCookie('user');

Choosing the Right Storage Option

Each storage method serves a different purpose:

  • : Best for storing data that you want to persist over long periods, such as user preferences.
  • : Ideal for data that should only be available during a single session, such as temporary form data.
  • : Useful when you need to send data back to the server with HTTP requests or when working with authentication tokens (keeping in mind the data size limitations).

When building your web application, consider the nature and lifetime of the data you’re storing. For example, if your application needs to remember user preferences even after closing the browser, LocalStorage is your go-to. Conversely, if you need data only during the session, SessionStorage is more appropriate. Conclusion

Understanding the differences between LocalStorage, SessionStorage, and Cookies is essential for effective client-side data management in JavaScript. Each method has its unique benefits and limitations:

_LocalStorage_ provides persistent storage across sessions.

_SessionStorage_ offers temporary storage within a single session.

_Cookies_ enable small data transfers between the client and server.

By choosing the appropriate storage solution, you can optimize your web application's performance and ensure that data is handled in the most efficient manner. Experiment with these examples and see how they can enhance the user experience on your site!
