items.title: Leveraging AWS API Destination to Trigger External APIs
items.author: Benjamin Ajewole
items.content:
Last year, while developing an AWS solution to integrate Salesforce and Microsoft Dynamics NAV, I was tasked with designing a system to capture DynamoDB stream updates and send them to both platforms. During this process, I discovered AWS API Destinations, which provided a seamless way to securely invoke external APIs, simplifying API call management and authentication.

AWS EventBridge API Destinations allow users to configure rules that securely send events to external APIs. They improve reliability by automatically retrying failed requests based on built-in retry policies. This article explores how to use API Destinations, referencing a high-level architecture diagram.

High-Level Architecture explanation

The architecture consists of the following components:

  • : Acts as the primary data store.

  • Stream Handler (AWS Lambda): This listens for changes in the DynamoDB stream and sends updates to EventBridge.

  • : Routes events to API Destination.

  • : Captures logs for monitoring and debugging.

  • : Acts as an intermediary to send requests to external APIs.

  • : Captures failed event deliveries for further analysis.

  • : Manages credentials for secure API authentication.

  • : Defines the authorization method, credentials, and network connectivity for EventBridge to communicate securely with the external webhook.

  • : The final external API endpoint that receives the triggered request.

Important AWS Considerations

  • Only Public APIs Supported: API Destinations support only public domain names with publicly trusted certificates for HTTPS endpoints. Private APIs that are not publicly resolvable and mutual TLS (mTLS) authentication are not supported.

  • : Requests to API Destinations must complete within 5 seconds. If the target endpoint takes longer, EventBridge will time out the request and retry it according to the maximums set in the configured retry policy.

Use Cases for AWS API Destination

  • Third-party Service Integration: You can easily integrate AWS event-driven architectures with SaaS platforms like Salesforce, Slack, or Stripe.

  • Webhook-based Notifications: Automate event-driven notifications to external services.

  • Cross-Cloud Communications: Bridge AWS events with APIs hosted on other cloud providers.

  • Security and Compliance Monitoring: Send security logs to third-party SIEM tools for real-time analysis.

  • Automated Data Synchronization: Push updates from AWS services to external databases or applications.

Best Practices for Using API Destination

  • Optimize API Response Time: Since API Destination has a 5-second timeout, ensure that the external API is optimized for quick responses. Use caching or lightweight processing where possible.

  • Utilize Dead Letter Queues (DLQ): Configure DLQs to capture failed API calls for debugging and retrying later.

  • Monitor with CloudWatch Logs: Enable logging to track API calls, errors, and response times for troubleshooting and optimization.

  • Leverage AWS Secrets Manager: Store API keys and authentication credentials securely instead of hardcoding them in API Destination configurations.

  • : To prevent exceeding third-party API limits, use throttling mechanisms or request prioritization strategies.

  • Use Exponential Backoff for Retries: Configure EventBridge retry policies to use exponential backoff, reducing the likelihood of overloading external APIs.

  • : Before integrating with a production API, use mock APIs to validate EventBridge configurations and request formatting.

  • Implement Security Best Practices: Restrict access to API Destinations by using IAM roles and policies to prevent unauthorized access.

  • Use Any HTTP Method Except CONNECT and TRACE: API Destination supports various HTTP methods, excluding CONNECT and TRACE.

  • Leverage Input Transformers: Customize event payloads to match the expected parameters of the external API endpoint, ensuring seamless integration.

  • Understand EventBridge Retry Behavior: Amazon EventBridge retries requests that receive HTTP error codes 401, 407, 409, 429, and any 5xx errors. It does not retry requests with 1xx, 2xx, 3xx, or most 4xx errors, except for the specified ones.

// Create a secret for client credentials
const clientSecret = new Secret(scope, 'SecretName', {
    secretName: 'secret-name',
});

// Create a secure connection for API authentication
const connection = new Connection(stack, 'ConnectionId', {
    connectionName: 'MyConnection',
    authorization: Authorization.oauth({
        authorizationEndpoint: "authorization-url",
        clientId: "client-id",
        clientSecret: clientSecret.secretValue,
        httpMethod: HttpMethod.POST,
        bodyParameters: {
            grant_type: "client_credentials",
        },
    }),
});

// Define the API Destination
const apiDestination = new ApiDestination(stack, 'ApiDestinationId', {
    apiDestinationName: 'ApiDestination',
    connection,
    endpoint: "endpoint-url",
    httpMethod: HttpMethod.POST,
    rateLimitPerSecond: 10,
});

// Create a Dead Letter Queue (DLQ) for failed events
const deadLetterQueue = new Queue(stack, 'DLQ');

// Define an EventBridge Rule
new Rule(stack, "EventBridgeRuleId", {
    ruleName: "EventBridgeRuleId",
    eventBus: new EventBus(stack, 'MyEventBus'),
    eventPattern: {
        source: ["eventSourceName"],
        detailType: ["eventDetailType"],
    },
    targets: [
        new ApiDestinationTarget(apiDestination, {
            deadLetterQueue,
            event: { detail: { key: "value" } },
            retryAttempts: 185,
            maxEventAge: Duration.hours(24),
        }),
    ],
});

AWS API Destination simplifies the process of securely triggering external APIs based on AWS events. Developers can build robust and scalable event-driven integrations with external services by leveraging managed authentication, retry policies, and monitoring capabilities. However, when designing solutions with API Destinations, it's essential to be mindful of execution time limits and supported API types.

items.title: Conectando Frontend React con APIs Serverless en AWS con GraphQL
items.author: Gustavo Ramirez
items.content:
Las aplicaciones modernas necesitan ser rápidas, escalables y eficientes en el manejo de datos. Una de las combinaciones más potentes para lograr esto es utilizar GraphQL con AWS AppSync en el backend y React en el frontend. En este artículo vamos a ver cómo crear un backend serverless con GraphQL utilizando AppSync y cómo conectar el frontend con Apollo Client. Además, cubriremos la autenticación con AWS Cognito para asegurar nuestras rutas.

¿Por qué utilizar AWS AppSync y GraphQL?

AWS AppSync es un servicio de GraphQL totalmente gestionado que facilita la creación de APIs flexibles y en tiempo real. Al usar AppSync, podemos conectar directamente nuestras aplicaciones frontend a servicios serverless, bases de datos como DynamoDB e incluso integrarlo con Lambda para lógica de negocio.

  • Consultas y mutaciones rápidas mediante GraphQL.
  • Actualizaciones en tiempo real con suscripciones.
  • Autenticación y autorización robustas con AWS Cognito.
  • Integración nativa con otros servicios de AWS.

Paso 1: Configurar el Backend con AWS AppSync

Crear un Proyecto con Amplify CLI

npm  @aws-amplify/cli

Agregar el Servicio AppSync

Añade GraphQL a tu proyecto:

  • Default configuration

Paso 2: Definir el Esquema de GraphQL

Vamos a crear un esquema básico para manejar tareas.

Ejecuta el siguiente comando para desplegar la API:

Esto generará el endpoint de AppSync y la configuración de GraphQL en el archivo .

Paso 3: Configuración del Frontend con Apollo Client

Instala Apollo Client y Amplify en tu proyecto React:

npm  @apollo/client graphql aws-amplify

Configurar Amplify en React

Agrega la configuración en el archivo :

Paso 4: Crear Consultas y Mutaciones

Crear la Mutación en Apollo

Paso 5: Implementar el Frontend

Buenas Prácticas de Estructuración

  1. Modularización del Código: Organiza consultas y mutaciones en directorios específicos.
  2. Autenticación Centralizada: Maneja los tokens en un contexto o servicio compartido.
  3. Utiliza tipos para garantizar la integridad de los datos.
  4. Implementa manejadores para los errores comunes de Apollo y GraphQL.

Con AWS AppSync y React, podemos construir aplicaciones rápidas y escalables utilizando GraphQL. La integración con Apollo Client facilita el consumo de datos en tiempo real y la autenticación con Cognito garantiza la seguridad del acceso. ¡Implementa esta combinación y lleva tu aplicación a otro nivel!

items.title: The Cultural Juggernaut That Redefined Gaming
items.author: Justin D
items.content:
When it comes to talk of gaming, few franchises have left their mark quite like Call of Duty. Since breaking onto the screne back on October 29, 2003, this first-person shooter has not only defined a genre but also cultivated a global following that has collectively logged over 25 billion hours—equivalent to 2.85 million years—immersed in its high-stakes combat. To put that in perspective, that’s longer than human civilisation itself. This mind-boggling statistic underscores the sheer magnitude of Call of Duty’s influence on modern culture, transforming gaming from a pastime into a global phenomenon.

The Evolution of a Gaming Titan

What began as a World War II-themed shooter in 2003 has since evolved into a franchise spanning multiple eras, narratives, and technological advancements. The series’ ability to adapt, innovate, and push graphical boundaries has kept players engaged for decades. Titles like Call of Duty: Modern Warfare and Black Ops have become household names, each iteration raising the bar for immersive gameplay, cinematic storytelling, and realistic mechanics.

One of the biggest factors behind the franchise’s enduring success is its ability to captivate a diverse audience. Whether it’s casual gamers looking for quick adrenaline-fueled matches or professional eSports players striving for perfection, Call of Duty offers something for everyone. The introduction of battle royale modes, such as Warzone, further cemented its dominance, drawing millions into the world of large-scale, tactical warfare.

From Game to Global eSports Sensation

Beyond casual play, Call of Duty has played a pivotal role in shaping the modern eSports landscape. Competitive gaming tournaments, such as the Call of Duty League (CDL), have turned skilled players into professional athletes, earning substantial prize pools and global recognition. Streaming platforms like Twitch and YouTube Gaming have further amplified the game’s reach, with top players and content creators amassing millions of followers and transforming their passion into lucrative careers.

A Cultural and Social Powerhouse

The influence of Call of Duty extends beyond the screen, shaping social interactions and online communities. Multiplayer lobbies have become digital meeting places where friendships are forged, rivalries are born, and teamwork is essential. Clans and squads transcend borders, uniting players from different backgrounds under a shared love for virtual combat. Additionally, the game has influenced mainstream media, inspiring Hollywood films, merchandise, and even military training simulations.

The Future of Call of Duty

With each new release, the franchise continues to evolve, leveraging cutting-edge technology such as AI-driven gameplay mechanics, ray-traced graphics, and cross-platform integration. As gaming technology advances, Call of Duty remains at the forefront, setting trends that shape the industry’s future. Whether it’s the next-gen console experience or the ever-expanding world of mobile gaming, the legacy of Call of Duty is far from over.

The staggering amount of time dedicated to Call of Duty is a testament to its unparalleled entertainment value, competitive spirit, and cultural impact. As gaming solidifies its place as a dominant form of entertainment, Call of Duty stands as a beacon of its potential—bridging generations, fostering communities, and redefining how we engage with digital worlds. Whether you’re a casual player, a competitive eSports athlete, or simply an admirer of interactive storytelling, one thing is certain: Call of Duty is more than a game—it’s a legacy in the making.

For more articles like this one click here.

“Nature has given us all the pieces required to achieve exceptional wellness and health, but has left it to us to put these pieces together.” —Diane McLaren

items.title: Mejorando la Experiencia de Usuario con Notificaciones Push en Aplicaciones Web Usando AWS SNS
items.author: Gustavo Ramirez
items.content:
Las notificaciones push son una herramienta poderosa para mantener a los usuarios actualizados en tiempo real sin necesidad de recargar la página. Integrarlas en aplicaciones web utilizando AWS Simple Notification Service (SNS) y AWS Lambda nos permite gestionar eventos de manera eficiente y escalable. En este artículo, veremos cómo implementar notificaciones push desde el frontend aprovechando el ecosistema de AWS.

¿Por qué Usar Notificaciones Push con AWS SNS?

AWS SNS es un servicio de mensajería totalmente gestionado que facilita el envío de mensajes a dispositivos móviles, correos electrónicos y servicios HTTP/HTTPS. Combinándolo con AWS Lambda, podemos automatizar el envío de notificaciones en respuesta a eventos específicos.

Beneficios de la implementación:

  • Notificaciones en tiempo real sin recarga de página.
  • Integración con múltiples servicios backend usando Lambda.
  • Escalabilidad automática y baja latencia.
  • Manejo eficiente de eventos en la nube.
  1. Frontend (React o Angular) para recibir las notificaciones push.
  2. para la gestión de mensajes y distribución.
  3. para procesar los eventos y enviar las notificaciones.

Paso 1: Configuración de AWS SNS

Primero, crearemos un tópico en SNS para manejar las notificaciones push.

Ejecuta el siguiente comando para crear el tópico:

aws sns create-topic  pushNotifications

Configuración de Permisos

Es importante asegurarse de que el tópico permita recibir mensajes desde Lambda:

aws sns set-topic-attributes  arn:aws:sns:us-east-1:123456789012:pushNotifications  Policy

Paso 2: Crear la Función Lambda

AWS Lambda procesará los eventos y enviará las notificaciones a SNS.

Vamos a crear una función que publique mensajes en el tópico SNS:

aws lambda create-function  pushNotificationHandler  nodejs20.x  arn:aws:iam::123456789012:role/lambda-execution-role  index.handler  fileb://function.zip

Código de la Función (index.js)

Sube la función a AWS Lambda:

aws lambda update-function-code  pushNotificationHandler  fileb://function.zip

Paso 3: Configuración del Frontend para Recibir Notificaciones

Registro del Service Worker (React)

Suscripción desde el Frontend

Paso 4: Disparar Notificaciones desde el Backend

Podemos desencadenar una notificación enviando una solicitud a Lambda:

aws lambda invoke  pushNotificationHandler 
  response.json

Buenas Prácticas de Seguridad

  1. Asegúrate de que las funciones Lambda tengan permisos mínimos.
  2. Implementa verificaciones para evitar abusos de notificaciones.
  3. Control de Suscripciones: Maneja las suscripciones de los usuarios de manera segura y eficiente.
  4. Verificación de Identidad: Utiliza tokens seguros en el frontend para identificar dispositivos.

Implementar notificaciones push usando AWS SNS y Lambda brinda una arquitectura escalable y eficiente para aplicaciones web modernas. Las notificaciones en tiempo real mejoran la experiencia del usuario, manteniéndolos informados sin necesidad de recargar la página.

¿Tienes dudas o quieres compartir tu experiencia? ¡Deja tus comentarios!

items.title: Reverse Engineering Third-Party Integrations — What API Docs Won’t Tell You
items.author: Prafulla Agarwal
items.content:
As developers, we love documentation.

But what happens when you work with

multiple third-party partners, and only some of them even docs?

At Stylework, I’ve had to integrate our system with various coworking space providers — each with their own quirks, limitations, and surprises.

This post shares my story of how I reverse-engineered partner APIs and built a stable system out of chaos.

🧩 The Reality of Third-Party APIs

Let’s break the illusion first — working with partners in the real world looked like this:

❌ Inconsistent or Missing Documentation

  • Some partners had API docs.
  • Some sent Google Sheets instead 🤯

I had to inspect frontend requests, intercept traffic, or just request payloads.

❌ Different Booking Flows

  • Some partners had a well-defined slot & booking process.
  • Others had no clear process — just a “make it work” attitude.

I had to reconstruct business logic based on scattered clues.

❌ No Dashboard Visibility

  • Some partners had dashboards where we could verify bookings.
  • Others? Nothing. Just a blind call-and-hope approach.

This made debugging 10x harder. We built internal logging and test harnesses to simulate every step.

❌ IP Whitelisting & Security Blocks

  • Some APIs blocked all IPs by default.
  • We had to request whitelisting for each environment (local, staging, prod).
  • Others had open endpoints with zero security 😅

Every integration had different auth rules — from basic tokens to custom headers.

❌ Architecture Differences

  • One API sent all available slots for a workspace.
  • Another sent one slot at a time per date.
  • A third stored everything as open/close timings.

We had to write for each architecture.

❌ Booking Confirmations? Maybe.

  • Some sent confirmation emails after a booking.
  • Some just reflected the change silently on a dashboard.

We couldn’t rely on notifications — so we wrote .

🔧 My Go-To Tactics for Reverse Engineering

Here’s what helped me survive and succeed:

  • 🛠 to watch real-time requests
  • 📄 Compare success vs failure payloads to learn edge cases
  • 🧪 when partners are unresponsive
  • 🧰 Create abstraction layers so the rest of our codebase doesn’t suffer
  • 📝 — especially partner-side responses
  • 💬 with partners, even if they’re not technical

Reverse engineering third-party APIs is messy, frustrating, and unpredictable.

  • Write flexible integration layers

You’ll unlock a that’s rarely taught, but always needed.

Have you ever worked with APIs that made you scream internally? 😅

Share your integration war stories in the comments 👇 — I’d love to hear them.

🧑‍💻 Full-Stack Developer | Node.js • Angular • DevOpsCurrently building real-time systems @Stylework

items.title: OpenAI Agents SDK + Multiple MCP Servers
items.author: Kazuhiro "Kaz" Sera
items.content:
// Detect dark theme var iframe = document.getElementById('tweet-1904957755829481737-874'); if (document.body.className.includes('dark-theme')) { iframe.src = "https://platform.twitter.com/embed/Tweet.html?id=1904957755829481737&theme=dark" }

The examples in the Agents SDK repository do not yet include one that runs multiple MCP servers at a time, so let's walk through a simple code demonstration of how to do that. In this quick guide, we'll use two MCP servers:

  1. Filesystem MCP Server: allows the agent to interact with your local file system.
  2. Slack MCP Server: enables the agent to interact with your Slack workspace.

First, ensure you have the required dependencies:

pip  openai-agents
npm  npx

Also, if you try the Slack MCP server out, you need to create a Slack app (=Slack integration) and pass its bot token and installed workspace's team ID.

Step 1: Head to https://api.slack.com/apps, click "Create New App", select "From a manifest", select your workspace, switch to YAML, and then past the following YAML data (you can change the app name if you want):

Step 2: Go to Settings > Install App; click the "Install to (workspace name)" button to enable the app in your Slack workspace and then set the following two variables on the terminal to run your agent app:

xoxb-....

T....

Step 3: Invite "Test MCP Connector" to the channels you want to read/write via MCP

Now, you're ready for your first agentic workflow with multiple MCP servers!

Here is a complete Python code to run. You can create a Python script named and then run . Please note that pip install -U openai-agents and should be done beforehand.

If everything works fine, you will see the output like below. I tested with "Can you help me find where app.py is, and also let me know the summary of the last 10 hours in #cat-lovers (C06CS8GRPC2)?" . You can use either of the two MCP servers for a prompt or both.

$ python app.py

Secure MCP Filesystem Server running on stdio
Allowed directories: [ '/Users/seratch/tmp/mcp-sample' ]
Starting Slack MCP Server...
Connecting server to transport...
Slack MCP Server running on stdio
View trace: https://platform.openai.com/traces/trace_*****************

Enter a prompt (MCP servers (fs, slack) are available): Can you help me find where app.py is, and also let me know the summary of the last 10 hours in #cat-lovers (C06CS8GRPC2)?

Received ListToolsRequest

Received CallToolRequest: {
  method: 'tools/call',
  params: {
    name: 'slack_get_channel_history',
    arguments: { channel_id: 'C06CS8GRPC2', limit: 100 }
  }
}

The `app.py` file is located at:

- `/Users/seratch/tmp/app.py`

### Summary from #cat-lovers in the last 10 hours:

1. **User <@U08KR020GLA> joined the channel.**

2. **User <@U03E94MK0> shared:**
   - Tried doing yoga, but the cat assumed it was “attack the moving limbs” hour 🧘‍♂️🧶
   - Mentioned their cat zooming around the house like she’s late for a nap 🐈💨
   - Reminded everyone that every keyboard serves as a cat bed, intended or not 🐾🧶⌨️

Feel free to ask if you need more information or any further assistance!

If you're curious, the reason I included the channel ID is that when there are many channels in a workspace, the default MCP server seems unable to identify a channel without an ID. If you implement your own MCP server that uses API response pagination, it should be able to find a channel just by its name, although it may take longer.

Tracing feature is batter included in the Agents SDK! So, you will be able to check detailed tracing of the workflow execution like below:

I hope this post was helpful to you. Using MCP servers can enable your agentic workflows to have more capabilities without implementing a lot on your own. Enjoy coding your agents to interact with MCP servers!

items.title: How to use Gyroscope in Presentations or Why Take a JoyCon to DPG2025
items.author: Kirill Vasin
items.content:
Animations, , especially the kind that were popular in PowerPoint 15–20 years ago, have largely taken a backseat. Add to this the compatibility issues between LibreOffice and MS Office (even between versions for Windows and Mac), the presence or absence of necessary fonts — and the desire to do something unusual on the "stage" fades away quickly.

Have a look at modern technical presentations: quite often, it's just a PDF document consisting of pages with vector and raster graphics, and sometimes GIF animations that eat up megabytes (like this post), with no mercy.

It's worth separating decorative from those that carry additional information in some media format. For example, take a look at ECMA-363 specification.

A 3D model inside a PDF document simply enhances the user/viewer experience. You observe the object from different angles/cross-sections.

It's disappointing that such a is almost nowhere supported except for Adobe Acrobat and likely will not be. It feels like we made a leap in the past, but now we have returned to static slides.

Large Scientific Conference DPG

— is a large European physics conference organized by the German Physical Society (DPG). Every year, they gather more than scientists and take place in German cities, covering a vast array of physics fields.

Spring Conference (Condensed Matter)

DPG2025 (Spring Meeting) took place in the wonderful city of Regensburg / REGENSBURG.MAPS

There are so many presentations, and it lasts almost a week, so by the end, it becomes too overwhelming. Nevertheless, this does not diminish its value as a platform for networking, practicing presentations, and a reliable way to learn what is currently on the market, which trains have gone already, and which are just departing.

The participants in plenary sessions are mostly Master's students and PhD students, with postdocs being rarer.

Such a large and accessible platform is an excellent motivation to try something new 💡 even if something may go wrong.

Surely, the reader has seen devices like this:

Logitech PPT Clicker / Logitech.com

This device acts as a slide switcher and sometimes as a laser pointer, connecting via Bluetooth or through a . In any case, it is a type of controller with buttons. Controllers can be more interesting — like the one from the 2017 Nintendo Switch handheld console

It is not much bigger, but it has some additional cool :

  • IR camera 📸 (difficult to use, no good API documentation)
  • Full IMU 🌐 (Inertial Measurement Unit) aka gyroscope with an accelerometer
  • Bluetooth connectivity; recognized as a regular HID

The buttons can indeed be mapped to PowerPoint, or the stick can be used to control slides, emulating mouse or keyboard clicks, as it was implemented in these projects:

I thought it would be cool to somehow use the IMU and analog stick. But for that, one would need to go beyond PowerPoint and PDF 🧙🏼‍♂️

Moving Slides to Browser Environment

The idea of behind this is not new, but it's important to remember that this approach may not work for everyone. However, by moving the presentation display and creation to the browser (particularly Javascript and HTML lands), we automatically gain access to all the possibilities of modern web technology: peripheral device support, JavaScript, CSS animation magic, and much more, including video. It's important to note that all of this is cross-platform by default and will work almost everywhere.

For example, slides can be created in Markdown (or/and HTML) with the help of a simple framework (or rather, a small library) — RevealJS

There is also MDX-based presentation engines, things like Manim, Motion Canvas, but these guys require even more skills to master.

The API is quite simple, so controlling the slides via JavaScript commands is easy to implement:

However, this direct approach has significant drawbacks. It requires an internet connection, and if you'd prefer to avoid it, you’ll need to use bundlers (such as Rollup) and embed all JavaScript libraries into a single HTML file, for instance. Alternatively, you could run a local web server.

Option with Jupyter Notebook

If you like Python and IPYNB, then use nbconvert — it will convert your notebook directly into a RevealJS presentation, and you won’t even notice it! Or, even simpler, use the extension for Jupyter — RISE

In any case, the idea remains simple — we need to somehow enter the web browser environment to take advantage of all the possibilities of JoyCon.

Option with WLJS Notebook

My opinion on WLJS might be somewhat biased as I am one of its developers (and active users). This open-source IDE with a notebook interface is more tightly integrated with the web environment, as slides are not exported there but are instead and are just another type of output cell, alongside the familiar Markdown.

Under the hood, it also uses RevealJS but with a few extra features:

  1. It allows embedding interactive elements and components, similar to LaTeX Beamer
  2. It is integrated with Wolfram Language (freeware distribution)

So, the easiest option is to use the already ready-made library joy-con-webhid. Why spend time reinventing the wheel when people have already done a great job for us?

npm joy-con-webhid –prefix

All subsequent examples will be taken from the WLJS Notebook. However, you can do pretty much the same thing using Python + FAST API to interface with JavaScript or something similar, or even just use JS alone. The online version of the notebook is available here.

First, let’s listen to what’s coming from the controller port.

The most important function here is:

It looks like there are many steps to do. In reality, most of this code deals with connecting the controller and drawing a large "Connect" button. Don’t pay too much attention to the special methods — they can easily be replaced with those available in your specific environment:

  • passes a for embedding on the page

  • calls when the cell is deleted, to clean up timers, etc.

  • The first line is a way to specify the JavaScript cell subtype in WLJS Notebook, which requires pre-bundling.

When we run this code cell, we will see the following:

  1. Disconnect the controller from the Nintendo Switch (System → Controllers → Disconnect).
  2. Pair the JoyCon (R) with the PC by holding the small button on the side.
  3. Press "Connect" on our .

Opening the browser console, we reveals the following messages:

Quite a lot of data! Let’s try using this for the benefit of our presentation 💡

To begin, we can use two buttons to switch slides

In the WLJS notebook, slides can also be controlled programmatically through a Wolfram wrapper function that calls the RevealJS API

All that's left is to trigger this function at the right moment when the button (or switch) is clicked. To do this, events need to be sent from the Javascript world to the Wolfram machine, where we can then do whatever we want with them. This results in the following diagram:

You don't have to think about this, since it is seamlessly implemented via APIs

Let's go back to the code cell and .

As you can see, we have added several items here:

  • - analog sticks drift, so their digital position is never perfect .
  • - why hammer the door every time if you only need to gently knock when the state changes? This reduces system stress.
  • Sending states to the event pool - this is specific to WLJS, where we send data to the Wolfram machine (or Python if you’re in Jupyter).

The last point looks like this (replace with the equivalent in your environment):

Then, on the Wolfram side, we can easily subscribe to these events like this:

This is very convenient, as Javascript sends the names of the pressed buttons as the pattern. In this case, you can immediately subscribe to slide switching, for example, like this:

    Thus, programmatically controlling slides becomes intuitive:

    Let’s create a simple presentation. Start typing with

    .slide

    Now, let’s connect the JoyCon to the PC and link it to our Javascript script by pressing the Connect button.

    Then, subscribe to the events in the active session:

    Now, just run the cell with the slides.

    The first big step in mastering JoyCon has been made!

    The stick theoretically allows controlling two sliders simultaneously. For the DPG Spring Meetings, I had the idea of a live demonstration of a very peculiar effect m𝒶𝑔𝒾c𝑎𝓁 𝓌𝑜𝓇𝒹𝓈 𝒻𝓇𝑜𝓂 𝓅𝒽𝓎𝓈𝒾𝒸𝓈. I believe that some concepts are much more impactful and comprehensible when demonstrated live .

    Here is a condensed code snippet for the interactive widget:

    .slide


Here it is in action

    Now, let's link it to our stick

    To begin with, let's perform a simple test and bind its position to a disk on the screen:

    Obviously, the movements are too abrupt. Moreover, making small adjustments is kinda painful using JoyCon. The solution?

    IMU or Gyroscope-Accelerometer Combination 🌐

    To use them, we need to read the corresponding fields from the object, namely:

    • for the accelerometer
    • () for the gyroscope

    Since IMU is not always needed, the script includes a boolean variable and a control function , allowing IMU measurements to be enabled or disabled.

    The JoyCon, like most other devices with IMU (some smartphones, watches, but definitely not VR headsets or quadcopters), includes:

    Question: Why can't we use only a gyroscope or an accelerometer?

    Let's try outputting both. First, enable IMU usage:

    Now, define auxiliary functions and variables:

    • The accelerometer vector is simply stored in .
    • Gyroscope data is processed by:
      • Reordering angular velocity values (JoyCon hardware orientation) and adjusting directions.
      • Integrating over time to obtain orientation angles.
    1. defining JoyCon orientation .
    2. (at rest - the gravity direction) .

    These three angles are conveniently expressed as a matrix (tensor):

    Applying this matrix to any 3D object allows it to be oriented according to these angles. Physically, on the JoyCon, it looks like this:

    It is important to note that since we measure only the first derivative (using Gyro), then the initial IMU orientation remains unknown. Therefore, we manually set the initial state .

    Well... Not quite obvious what these values mean. Let's try to draw then as vectors in 3D space

    And then render them as colored cones

    1. - Gyro angles

    2. - accelerometer direction (inverted actually)

    The green vector is always aligned "correctly," while the blue and red vectors, representing gyroscope angles, accumulate errors over time, especially with rapid movements, causing .

    There are many ways to solve this issue. The general idea is to adjust the angles using accelerometer data (green vector), as the accelerometer precisely determines the downward direction (until an external force disturbs it).

    Why the heck do we need this in the presentation?

    I asked myself this question when I discovered how deep the rabbit hole goes. In my talk at the magnetism session, there was exactly one slide where the idea of an IMU made any sense.

    Do you see the crystalline structure? Finding a "good" camera angle for it is indeed difficult, so why not rotate it directly, ? We don’t need all three angles and acceleration—just one will suffice.

    Thus, we discard the accelerometer and keep only the gyroscope.

    Now, we just need to apply the transformation tensor to our 3D structure. Since this crystal contains many ions, which are also colored, I compressed them into base64.

    Let's embed it into our slide.

    .slide



Here is my crystal structure!

    To make it even more convenient, it would be useful to subscribe to IMU only when the slide is active and unsubscribe when leaving it. This is easy to do because RevealJS signals the core on slide state change events. Let’s implement this as a component using cell type:

    By placing this code on any slide, we achieve the desired result without polluting the global space or interfering with other event handlers. Thus, IMU subscription management is localized to the specific slide, and when switching slides, we correctly enable and disable data handling without affecting other slides and their processing.

    .slide



Here is my crystal structure!

    No evaluation is required (don't run it ;)). Simply hide the input cell, leaving only the output visible through the properties (top-right corner of the group).

    Here is a (some of which work in a browser as well without a kernel).

    What if using my own laptop is not an option?

    The WLJS Notebook can be exported to HTML, preserving even some dynamic behavior. This is achieved through a quite sophisticated algorithm that tracks all event chains occurring on the JS and WL sides, attempting to approximate them using a simple state machine. The result is a standard HTML file containing all cells, slides, and the data of these state machines.

    However, due to the variety of values received from the IMU, the exporter cannot automatically capture this in a JS state machine. Consequently, rotations and the joystick input will not be preserved. Nonetheless, everything else will function as expected. 🙂

    ... like a fish needs a bicycle

    src. almost a public domain

    If not for the urgent need to showcase a rotating crystalline structure, this post would not exist.

    Thank you for your attention, and to those who read this far. 🧙🏼‍♂️ 🤍

items.title: Exploring ChatGPT’s New Image Generation Feature: Studio Ghibli-Inspired Artwork
items.author: Gajanan Rajput

items.title: My 48h SaaS Challenge
items.author: Bartolomeo Hart
items.content:
Just wrapped up a challenge to build a SaaS as quickly as possible 🚀

Create a working SaaS product that analyzes crypto content, solo from scratch, within the timespan of 2 days!

I spent the first 6 hours researching, planning and designing what to build and how to build it. With crypto being my focus area, I wanted something that could extract insights from videos and provide actionable data.

The frontend work consumed about 10 hours, building a responsive Next.js website with landing page and dashboard.

I was able to build a complete frontend containing: • A pricing overview page • Dark/light mode with persistence

• Full legal documentation

And much more, even dark/light mode. It's actually cool that it turned out this way with limited time!

The dashboard uses Chart.js for visualizations and lets users track price targets, follow event timelines, and filter through raw data, which can be exported as .csv and used for ones own purposes.

Backend development took roughly 6 hours, setting up FastAPI, YouTube caption extraction, and database integration. While I've built Flask and Django backends before, this was my first FastAPI project.

And that's where things got tricky. 😅

The FastAPI with background tasks became a nightmare, concurrent operations kept hanging. With only 4 hours left to debug, I couldn't solve the fundamental issues with the AI-generated boilerplate code.

Payment integration was another unexpected challenge. Cryptomus rejected my application twice, first for an incomplete offer (fair), then because "we do not work with this category of projects." Despite checking their guidelines thoroughly, I never found any mention of restrictions on crypto+AI projects.

The biggest lessons I'm taking away:

Don't experiment with new tech stacks during tight deadlines, especially AI-generated ones. Stick with what you know or learn new tools beforehand.

Payment integration needs more planning than I anticipated. Having a complete, polished product ready before applying to payment providers is crucial.

I spent too much time (again🫠) perfecting the frontend while the backend needed more attention, a classic mistake when racing against the clock.

Despite the challenges, I'm not abandoning CryptoGist. It's actually a tool I genuinely need myself! I'll keep developing it, try different payment processors like Coinbase Commerce, or maybe make it open source.

This 48-hour sprint taught me what's possible with modern tools, and eventhough there where some pitfalls, I believe that challenges such as these will allow you to become better and better with each iteration, allowing you to slowly setup a framework for success and a structured approach to quickly get stuff off the ground.

I like to try out stuff and build cool things, give me a follow on x.com if you want to see more of my failed attempts (and successes)👋

items.title: Forward vs. Reverse Proxy: A Developer Friendly Guide
items.author: Athreya aka Maneshwar
items.content:
When dealing with web infrastructure, proxies play a crucial role in managing traffic, securing connections, and optimizing performance.

However, not all proxies serve the same purpose. Forward proxies and reverse proxies may seem similar at first glance, but they serve different roles in a network.

Understanding their distinctions is essential for developers working on network security, scalability, and access control.

A proxy server is an intermediary between clients and servers, handling requests and responses.

Instead of direct communication between a client and a destination server, the proxy intercepts the request, processes it, and forwards it appropriately.

Depending on the type of proxy used, this intermediary role can serve different purposes.

A sits between client devices and the internet, acting on behalf of the client when making requests.

The client sends a request to the forward proxy, which then forwards it to the destination server.

The server's response follows the same path back to the client via the proxy.

Consider three entities involved in a typical forward proxy setup:

  • A user's computer or device
  • The intermediary that handles outgoing requests
  • The web server hosting the requested content
  1. Client A sends a request to Forward Proxy B.
  2. Forward Proxy B forwards the request to Destination Server C.
  3. Destination Server C responds to Forward Proxy B.
  4. Forward Proxy B relays the response back to Client A.

Forward proxies offer several advantages, especially for users and organizations needing control over internet access and security:

    • Some governments, workplaces, or schools impose firewalls that restrict access to certain websites.
    • Forward proxies allow users to route their traffic through an external server, effectively bypassing these restrictions.

  2. Enhancing Anonymity and Privacy:

    • Since all client requests are funneled through the proxy, websites only see the proxy's IP address rather than the user's actual IP.
    • This can help protect user identity and prevent tracking.

  3. Content Filtering and Monitoring:

    • Organizations can use forward proxies to restrict access to certain websites or categories of content, enforcing internet usage policies.

  4. Web Scraping and Data Aggregation:

    • Forward proxies enable large-scale data extraction by masking the origin of requests, preventing anti-scraping mechanisms from blocking access.
    • Users or businesses needing to test regional website variations or access geo-blocked content can use forward proxies to simulate being in different locations.

Forward proxies come in different forms based on their origin:

  • Use IPs provided by ISPs, making them appear as real users.
  • Use IPs from cloud or hosting providers, offering faster speeds but lower trust levels.

A sits in front of web servers, intercepting incoming client requests and forwarding them to appropriate backend servers.

Unlike a forward proxy, which represents clients, a reverse proxy acts on behalf of servers, optimizing performance, security, and reliability.

In a typical reverse proxy setup, we have:

  • A user accessing a website
  • The intermediary managing incoming traffic
  • The actual web server handling requests
  1. Client D sends a request to Reverse Proxy E.
  2. Reverse Proxy E forwards the request to Backend Server F.
  3. Backend Server F processes the request and sends a response to Reverse Proxy E.
  4. Reverse Proxy E relays the response back to Client D.

Reverse proxies are widely used to enhance web performance, security, and scalability. Some key benefits include:

    • Websites with high traffic can distribute incoming requests across multiple backend servers to prevent overload and ensure availability.

  2. Caching for Performance Optimization:

    • Frequently requested resources (such as images, videos, and static pages) can be cached by the reverse proxy, reducing response times and server load.
    • SSL/TLS encryption and decryption are resource-intensive. Reverse proxies can handle this process, freeing backend servers to focus on application logic.

  4. Security and Anonymity for Backend Servers:

    • Reverse proxies mask the actual IP addresses of backend servers, making it harder for attackers to target them directly.

  5. DDoS Protection and Traffic Filtering:

    • By acting as a buffer between clients and servers, reverse proxies can mitigate distributed denial-of-service (DDoS) attacks and filter out malicious traffic.

  6. Global Server Load Balancing (GSLB):

    • Reverse proxies can route user requests to geographically closest data centers, reducing latency and improving load times.

Common Reverse Proxy Solutions

Popular reverse proxy software includes:

  • – A powerful, high-performance reverse proxy and web server.
  • – A widely used solution for load balancing and high availability.

Forward Proxy vs. Reverse Proxy: Key Differences

Represents clients, forwarding requests to serversRepresents servers, managing requests from clients
Anonymity, bypassing restrictions, content filteringLoad balancing, caching, SSL termination, security
End users, corporate IT, web scrapersWeb administrators, DevOps teams, site reliability engineers
Protects backend infrastructure
Outbound (client → proxy → server)Inbound (client → proxy → server)
  • if you need to manage internet access, anonymize browsing, or bypass restrictions.
  • if you need to optimize web server performance, enhance security, or distribute traffic efficiently.

Both forward and reverse proxies play essential roles in modern web infrastructure.

While forward proxies help users navigate the internet securely and anonymously, reverse proxies ensure that websites remain scalable, secure, and performant.

Understanding their differences and use cases enables developers to make informed decisions when designing network architectures.

I’ve been working on a super-convenient tool called LiveAPI.

LiveAPI helps you get all your backend APIs documented in a few minutes

With LiveAPI, you can quickly generate interactive API documentation that allows users to execute APIs directly from the browser.

If you’re tired of manually creating docs for your APIs, this tool might just make your life easier.

items.title: A Comprehensive Guide to CSS Terminology
items.author: gerry leo nugroho
items.content:
CSS (Cascading Style Sheets) is the language that brings life to the web. It transforms plain HTML structures into visually stunning, interactive, and user-friendly websites. However, with its vast array of , , and , CSS can sometimes feel overwhelming—especially for beginners or developers revisiting the language after some time. From mastering the to understanding and , CSS terminology is both powerful and intricate.

But here’s the good news, once you break it down into manageable pieces, CSS becomes not only approachable but also incredibly fun to work with. Whether you’re building a simple landing page or a complex web application, understanding CSS terminology is the key to unlocking your creativity and ensuring your designs look great across all devices.

The primary goal of this post is to refresh and deepen your understanding of CSS terminology. We’ll cover the foundational concepts and practical applications in a way that’s easy to follow, even if you’re new to CSS. By breaking down complex topics like , , , and into bite-sized categories, we could aim to make CSS more accessible and actionable for developers at all levels.

This guide isn’t just about theory—it’s packed with real-world examples and code snippets you can immediately apply to your projects. Whether you’re debugging a tricky layout issue or designing a responsive form, this post will equip you with the tools and knowledge you need to succeed.

In this post, we’ll cover the following key areas:

  • : Understand the foundational concepts that govern how styles are applied, including the , , , and the .
  • : Learn the building blocks of CSS, such as , , , and , and how they work together to style HTML elements.
  • : Explore common CSS properties and their corresponding values, grouped into categories like , , , and .
  • : Discover techniques for styling images effectively and customizing forms to improve usability and aesthetics.
  • : Dive into mouse states like , , and to create dynamic and engaging user experiences.
  • Practical Examples and Implementation: Combine everything you’ve learned into complete, real-world examples that demonstrate how CSS works in action.

By the end of this post, you’ll have a solid grasp of CSS terminology and the confidence to tackle even the most challenging styling tasks. Let’s get started!

2. Core Principles of CSS

CSS operates based on foundational principles like , , , and . These principles determine how styles are applied and interact with each other, shaping the behavior of your web designs. Understanding these core concepts is essential for writing efficient, maintainable, and predictable CSS.

Whether you’re debugging a layout issue or optimizing for performance, mastering these principles will help you create clean and functional styles. In this chapter, we’ll explore each of these principles in detail, providing examples and actionable insights to solidify your understanding.

The is one of the most fundamental aspects of CSS. It determines how styles are applied based on their and . When multiple rules target the same element, the cascade decides which rule takes precedence. Here’s how it works:

  • : Later rules override earlier ones within the same source.
  • : Styles can come from three sources:
    • : Styles applied by the browser (e.g., default margins on paragraphs).
    • : Custom styles defined by the user (e.g., via browser settings or extensions).
    • : Styles written by the developer (you!).

: Understanding the cascade helps you predict and control how styles are applied, especially when dealing with conflicting rules.

In this example, all elements will appear red because the second rule overrides the first due to its position in the cascade.

is a priority system that determines which styles take precedence when multiple rules apply to the same element. It’s like a that ranks selectors based on their "weight." Here’s how specificity works:

  • :
    1. (e.g., ) have the highest specificity.
    2. (e.g., ) come next.
    3. , , and (e.g., , , ) follow.
    4. and (e.g., , ) have the lowest specificity.

: A more specific selector will always override a less specific one, regardless of the order in the stylesheet.

In this case, the element with the ID will appear red because IDs have higher specificity than classes.

: Avoid overusing IDs in your CSS to prevent specificity conflicts, as they can make your styles harder to maintain.

Some CSS properties are by from their . This means that if you define a on a , its will automatically unless . Inheritance helps reduce redundancy and ensures consistency across your design.

Common Inherited Properties:

    : Inheritance is particularly useful for typography and text-related styles, as it allows you to define global styles on the or elements and have them propagate throughout your document.

    In this example, every element inside the will use the Arial font unless another rule specifies otherwise.

    : Use the keyword to explicitly inherit a property from a parent element:

    The is a fundamental concept in CSS that defines how elements are structured and spaced within a layout. Every HTML element is treated as a rectangular box, consisting of four components:

    • : The innermost part of the box where text and images appear.
    • : The space between the content and the border.
    • : The outer edge of the padding.
    • : The space outside the border, separating the element from other elements.

    Understanding the box model is crucial for controlling layout and spacing effectively.

    +---------------------------+
|         Margin            |
|  +---------------------+  |
|  |       Border        |  |
|  |  +---------------+  |  |
|  |  |    Padding    |  |  |
|  |  |  +---------+  |  |  |
|  |  |  | Content |  |  |  |
|  |  |  +---------+  |  |  |
|  |  +---------------+  |  |
|  +---------------------+  |
+---------------------------+
    • The total width of the element is calculated as: 
    Width = Content Width + Padding (left + right) + Border (left + right)
Width = 200px + 20px (10px × 2) + 10px (5px × 2) = 230px

    : Use the property to include padding and borders in the element’s total width and height, making layouts easier to manage:

    CSS syntax is the foundation of how styles are written and applied to HTML documents. It consists of , , , and , which together form the building blocks of CSS. Understanding these elements is essential for writing clean, maintainable, and effective styles. In this chapter, we’ll break down each component of CSS syntax and provide practical examples to help you grasp how they work.

    Selectors are the tools that target specific HTML elements for styling. They allow you to apply styles to individual elements, groups of elements, or elements in specific states. Let’s explore the most common types of selectors:

    The targets elements by their tag name (e.g., , , ). It applies styles to all instances of the specified element.

    In this example, all elements will have navy-colored text.

    The targets elements based on their attribute. Classes are reusable and can be applied to multiple elements, making them ideal for styling components like buttons, cards, or sections.

    Here, any element with the class will have a blue background and white text.

    The targets a single unique element by its attribute. IDs are more specific than classes and should be used sparingly to avoid specificity conflicts.

    In this case, the element with the ID will have a dark gray background.

    The matches elements based on their attributes (e.g., , ). This is particularly useful for styling form inputs or other elements with specific attributes.

    Here, only elements with the attribute will have a gray border.

    A targets elements in specific states, such as when they’re hovered over (), clicked (), or matched by a certain pattern (). Pseudo-classes add interactivity and dynamic behavior to your styles.

    In this example, the button’s background color changes to green when the user hovers over it.

    A styles specific parts of an element, such as the first line of a paragraph () or content before/after an element (, ). Pseudo-elements are powerful for fine-grained control over styling.

    Here, the first line of every element will appear in bold.

    3.2 Declarations and Rulesets

    A consists of a that defines a specific style. A groups one or more declarations within a block, associated with a selector. Together, declarations and rulesets form the core structure of CSS.

    • A specifies what aspect of the element is being styled (e.g., , ).
    • A specifies the exact style to apply (e.g., , ).
    • The selector targets all elements.
    • The property sets the text color to blue.
    • The property sets the text size to 36 pixels.

    are special directives prefixed with that enable conditional and dynamic styling. They allow you to apply styles based on conditions, import external stylesheets, or define animations.

    • : Applies styles based on media queries (e.g., screen size).
    • : Defines animations.
    • : Imports external stylesheets.
    • The rule applies styles only when the viewport width is 768px or less.
    • The text size is reduced to 14px for better readability on smaller devices.

    CSS properties define of an element is being styled, while values specify to apply. There are hundreds of standardized properties, each with its own set of valid values. These properties control everything from typography and layout to colors, spacing, and animations. In this chapter, we’ll explore common CSS properties grouped into categories for clarity:

    • : Styling text for readability and aesthetics.
    • : Controlling the structure and alignment of elements.
    • : Adding visual appeal with static and dynamic colors.
    • : Using flexible units and calculations for responsive designs.

    By the end of this chapter, you’ll have a solid understanding of how to use these properties effectively in your projects.

    Typography is one of the most important aspects of web design. Properly styled text improves readability, accessibility, and overall user experience. Let’s look at some key properties for styling text:

    • : Specifies the typeface(s) to use.
    • : Defines the size of the text.
    • : Controls the spacing between lines of text.
    • : Aligns text horizontally (e.g., left, center, right).
    • The ensures a clean and readable typeface.
    • The sets a comfortable base size for body text.
    • The improves readability by adding vertical spacing.
    • The centers all text within the .

    CSS layout properties allow you to control the structure and alignment of elements on the page. Modern CSS provides powerful tools like and for creating responsive and dynamic layouts.

    • : Defines how an element behaves in the layout (e.g., , , , ).
    • : Controls the positioning of elements (e.g., , , , ).
    • : Enables flexible layouts with flexbox.
    • : Creates grid-based layouts with rows and columns.
    • The property enables flexbox.
    • The distributes child elements evenly across the container.
    • The vertically centers the child elements.

    4.3 Colors and Backgrounds

    Colors and backgrounds add visual appeal to your designs. CSS provides multiple ways to define colors, including static values, dynamic transparency, and variables.

    Static colors are defined using predefined formats like hex codes (), RGB (), or named colors ().

    • The sets a light gray background.
    • The defines dark gray text for contrast.

    Dynamic colors allow you to create effects like transparency or dynamically adjust colors using variables.

    • The function creates a semi-transparent red background.
    • CSS variables () allow you to define reusable color values.

    4.4 Units and Measurements

    CSS supports a variety of units for defining sizes, spacing, and other measurements. Understanding these units is crucial for creating responsive and scalable designs.

    Basic units include absolute units like and relative units like , , and .

    • The uses a percentage to make the box responsive.
    • The uses for consistent spacing based on the root font size.

    Modern CSS includes functions like , , , and for dynamic calculations and responsive designs.

    • The function performs mathematical operations.
    • The function ensures a responsive font size that scales within a range.
    • The function selects the larger value for spacing.

    Styling images is a common yet essential task in CSS. Whether you’re working with inline tags or using images as backgrounds, CSS provides powerful tools to control how images are displayed and integrated into your layouts. Properly styled images improve the aesthetics, responsiveness, and usability of your designs.

    In this chapter, we’ll explore two key techniques for styling images:

    • : Controls how an image fits within its container.
    • : Adds decorative visuals using and related properties.

    By the end of this chapter, you’ll have the tools to style images effectively and ensure they look great across all devices.

    The property controls how an image (or video) fits within its container. It’s particularly useful for responsive designs where images need to maintain their aspect ratio while fitting into different-sized containers.

    • : Stretches the image to fill the container, potentially distorting it.
    • : Scales the image to fit within the container while maintaining its aspect ratio.
    • : Scales the image to cover the entire container, cropping any overflow.
    • : Displays the image at its original size, ignoring the container dimensions.
    • : Scales the image down to fit within the container if necessary.
    • The ensures the image spans the full width of its container.
    • The sets a fixed height for the image.
    • The ensures the image scales proportionally to cover the container, cropping any overflow.

    : This technique is ideal for hero images, thumbnails, or gallery layouts where consistent sizing is important.

    Using allows you to add decorative visuals to elements like headers, sections, or buttons. Combined with properties like , , and , you can create visually appealing designs that adapt to different screen sizes.

    • : Specifies the image to use as a background.
    • : Controls the size of the background image (, , or specific dimensions).
    • : Aligns the image within the container (e.g., , , ).
    • : Determines whether the image repeats (, ).
    • The adds a decorative image to the section.
    • The ensures the image scales proportionally to cover the entire section.
    • The background-position: center centers the image within the container.
    • The background-repeat: no-repeat prevents the image from tiling.

    : This technique is perfect for creating hero sections, banners, or decorative backgrounds that enhance the overall design.

    Forms are a fundamental part of web development, enabling users to interact with websites by submitting data, logging in, or filling out surveys. Properly styled forms not only enhance the visual appeal of your website but also improve and . Well-designed forms guide users through the input process, reduce errors, and provide a seamless experience across devices.

    In this chapter, we’ll explore how to style common form elements like inputs, buttons, checkboxes, and dropdowns using CSS. We’ll cover:

    • Basic form styling for readability and aesthetics.
    • Focus states for better accessibility.
    • Customizing checkboxes and radio buttons for a polished look.
    • Aligning form elements neatly using flexbox or grid.
    • Making forms responsive for all screen sizes.

    By the end of this chapter, you’ll have the tools to create functional, visually appealing, and accessible forms.

    Styling basic form elements like text inputs, buttons, and labels ensures readability and improves the overall user experience. Consistent padding, font sizes, and borders make forms easier to use.

    Name:Submit

    : These styles create a clean and professional look for forms, making them easy to read and interact with.

    6.2 Focus States and Accessibility

    Focus states are essential for , as they highlight the currently active form element for users navigating with a keyboard. Using the pseudo-class, you can add visual cues like borders, outlines, or shadows to indicate focus.

    : Always ensure that focus states are visible and distinct. Avoid removing focus indicators entirely, as this can hinder users who rely on keyboard navigation.

    6.4 Customizing Checkboxes and Radio Buttons

    Default checkboxes and radio buttons often look outdated or inconsistent across browsers. By hiding the default styles and replacing them with custom designs, you can create a polished and cohesive look.

    
  Subscribe to newsletter

    : Customized checkboxes and radio buttons align with your design system and improve the overall aesthetic of your forms.

    6.5 Aligning Form Elements

    Aligning form elements neatly is crucial for creating a clean and professional layout. Tools like or make it easy to align labels, inputs, and buttons horizontally or vertically.

    Email:Submit

    : This approach ensures that labels and inputs are aligned consistently, improving readability and usability.

    6.6 Responsive Design for Forms

    Forms must look good and function well on all screen sizes, from desktops to mobile devices. Using media queries and relative units, you can adapt form layouts for smaller screens.

    • Use relative units like , , or for widths and spacing.
    • Ensure buttons and inputs are large enough to tap easily on touch devices (minimum 48px × 48px).
    • Test your forms on various devices to ensure usability.

    7. Adding Interactivity with Mouse States

    Mouse states (e.g., hover, active, focus) are a powerful way to add interactivity to your designs. These states allow you to respond to user actions, such as hovering over a button or clicking an input field, by applying dynamic styles. Incorporating these effects not only enhances the visual appeal of your website but also improves the overall user experience by providing feedback and guiding interactions.

    In this chapter, we’ll explore how to use mouse states effectively:

    • : Highlight elements when users hover over them.
    • : Provide feedback when elements are clicked or pressed.
    • : Improve accessibility by highlighting focused elements.

    By the end of this chapter, you’ll know how to make your designs more dynamic and user-friendly.

    The pseudo-class applies styles when a user hovers over an element. Hover effects are commonly used on buttons, links, and cards to indicate interactivity and draw attention.

    : Hover effects are ideal for buttons, navigation menus, and interactive elements. They provide visual feedback, encouraging users to interact with your design.

    : Avoid overusing hover effects on touch devices, as they may not work consistently. Instead, ensure that your design remains functional without relying solely on hover states.

    7.2 Active and Focus States

    The pseudo-class applies styles when an element is being actively clicked or pressed, while the pseudo-class highlights elements that currently have keyboard focus. These states are essential for creating responsive and accessible designs.

    • The state provides immediate feedback when a user interacts with an element, such as clicking a button.
    • The state ensures that keyboard users can easily identify which element is currently active, improving accessibility.

    : Always ensure that focus states are visible and distinct. Avoid removing focus indicators entirely, as this can hinder users who rely on keyboard navigation.

    Why These Techniques Matter

    1. : Mouse states provide feedback, making your website feel more responsive and engaging.
    2. : Focus states ensure that all users, including those using keyboards or assistive technologies, can navigate your site effectively.
    3. Consistency Across Devices: By combining hover, active, and focus states, you create a cohesive and intuitive experience for all users.

    For more information on pseudo-classes and their applications, refer to the official documentation:

    8. Putting It All Together

    Throughout this guide, we’ve explored the foundational principles of CSS, including syntax elements, properties, values, and interactivity. Now, it’s time to bring everything together into a complete, real-world example. This exercise will demonstrate how all the concepts you’ve learned—such as selectors, layout techniques, styling images, and adding interactivity—work harmoniously to create a polished and functional design.

    By combining these elements, you’ll see how CSS can transform a simple HTML structure into a visually appealing and interactive component.

    Below is a complete example of a styled card component. This example incorporates:

    • : Clean and readable fonts.
    • : Flexbox for centering and alignment.
    • : A consistent color scheme with subtle borders and backgrounds.
    • : Using to ensure images scale proportionally.
    • : Hover effects on buttons for a dynamic user experience. 
    Card TitleThis is a sample card with CSS styling.Click Me
    1. :
      • The font-family: 'Arial', sans-serif; ensures a clean and professional look for all text.
      • The centers the text within the card.
    2. :
      • The uses (, , ) to center the card both horizontally and vertically within the viewport.
      • The ensures the container spans the full height of the viewport.
    3. :
      • The has a light gray background (background-color: #f0f0f0) to provide contrast with the white card.
      • The uses a white background () and a subtle border () for definition.
    4. :
      • The and ensure the image scales proportionally while maintaining a consistent size.
      • The ensures the image fills the container without distortion.
      • The border-radius: 10px 10px 0 0 rounds the top corners of the image for a polished look.
    5. :
      • The includes a hover effect (background-color: #0056b3) that changes the background color when the user hovers over it.
      • The transition: background-color 0.3s ease adds a smooth animation for the hover effect.

    This chapter brings together everything you’ve learned so far, showing how CSS principles and techniques can be applied to create a functional and visually appealing design. By experimenting with this example and customizing it further, you’ll gain confidence in using CSS to build your own projects.

    CSS is a powerful language that brings structure, style, and interactivity to the web. Throughout this guide, we’ve broken down CSS terminology into manageable categories, making it easier to understand and apply in your projects. Here’s a quick recap of what we’ve covered:

    • : We explored foundational concepts like the cascade, specificity, inheritance, and the box model, which govern how styles are applied and interact.
    • : We learned about selectors, declarations, rulesets, and at-rules—the building blocks of CSS syntax.
    • : We delved into common properties for typography, layout, colors, and measurements, as well as advanced techniques like and CSS variables.
    • : We discussed how to style images effectively using and background properties, as well as how to create visually appealing and accessible forms.
    • : We added dynamic effects using mouse states like , , and to enhance user experience.
    • : Finally, we combined all these concepts into a complete example, demonstrating how CSS can transform a simple HTML structure into a polished and functional design.

    By breaking CSS into these categories, we’ve shown that even though CSS terminology is vast, it becomes much more approachable when tackled step by step.

    Now it’s your turn! CSS is a versatile language, and there’s always more to learn and experiment with. Here’s how you can take your knowledge further:

    • Share Your Favorite CSS Tips: What’s one CSS property or technique you couldn’t live without? Let us know in the comments below!
    • : If anything in this guide sparked curiosity or left you wondering, feel free to ask questions in the comments—we’re here to help!
    • : Take the examples from this post and customize them to fit your own projects. Try swapping out colors, layouts, or interactions to make them your own.

    Remember, mastering CSS is a journey, and every project is an opportunity to learn something new. Keep experimenting, keep building, and most importantly, have fun with it!

    To deepen your understanding of CSS and stay up-to-date with the latest techniques, here are some trusted external resources for further reading:

    • : The ultimate reference for all things CSS, with detailed explanations, examples, and browser compatibility information.

    • : A treasure trove of CSS tips, tricks, and tutorials, including the popular CSS Almanac.

    • : The official CSS specifications from the World Wide Web Consortium (W3C), providing authoritative guidelines for CSS standards.

    • Interactive Learning Platforms: Practice your CSS skills with interactive platforms like:

    These resources will help you expand your CSS knowledge and stay ahead of the curve as web development continues to evolve.

items.title: SQL Day 5: UNION, UNION ALL, Data Type Changes, and CAST in PostgreSQL
items.author: Vigneshwaralingam
items.content:
Today’s SQL learning session covered important concepts like UNION, UNION ALL, data type changes in PostgreSQL, and CAST operations. These topics are crucial when handling large datasets, managing data types, and ensuring accurate data retrieval. Additionally, I attended a , where I encountered some challenging SQL queries, which I’ll also discuss in this blog.

1. UNION vs UNION ALL in PostgreSQL

Both and are used to combine results from multiple queries, but they behave differently when handling duplicate values.

  • Combines results from multiple queries.

Sometimes, data in PostgreSQL is stored in the wrong format, and we need to convert it to the correct data type.

SQL Test – Challenging Queries

During my test, I encountered some tough SQL questions. Below are the queries and their solutions.

✅ Retrieves movies with both ImDB and Critique Rating < 7.

Today's SQL session was exciting! Learning about UNION, UNION ALL, casting data types, and removing duplicates is essential for managing databases efficiently. The test was challenging, but it helped reinforce my understanding of writing .

💡 , while UNION ALL retains all values.CAST and :: are used for type conversions in PostgreSQL.DISTINCT eliminates duplicate records.Complex SQL queries require careful conditions and joins.

items.title: Touch Events (swipe) using Stimulus
items.author: Rails Designer
items.content:
The time you build and design web-apps for the desktop, without touch events, has been long gone. Users expect features to work on their touch devices (phones, tablets) just as well.

In this article I want to explore two features where touch events can be used:

  • tinder-like, left- and right card swipes.

Something like this (view the gif in all its glory on the original article):

(don't hate for disliking the first cat!)

Because there is a lot of overlap with these two features I am also going to explore , meaning one Stimulus controller inherits functionality from another class (just like Ruby's UsersController < ApplicationController).

As most of the time with such features, let's start with the HTML as it helps to guide what functionality is needed (this HTML is using Tailwind CSS, but that is optional):


      Previous
    
      Next

Easy enough, right? The HTML is already telling pretty much how the controller will look like:

The and methods handle circular navigation in the carousel where ⁠ increments the and ⁠ decrements it, both using the modulo operator (⁠%) with ⁠the value from to wrap around to the beginning/end, so the always stays within valid bounds ().

Cool, right? A simple carousel that works on desktops! Now let's add support for touch devices. All that is needed in the HTML is the data-carousel-threshold-value and the :

Now extend the controller with and methods:

Okay, it needs two methods: and :

Alright, getting deeper into the rabbit hole, now those methods need a , and (let's also add the last getter-method that is needed):

If you try to swipe left/right on the carousel, on a real touch device or enabling in your developer console, you can navigate the images as well. Pretty cool (and the images too, right?)!

But let's not stop here! I also like to explore a Tinder-like feature where you swipe carts (cats + cards) left and right (should I build a carts.app?). As usual, first the HTML:


      No more carts… 😿

You notice the same and event listeners, but a different controller name, that is because I want to use inheritance that holds the reusable logic between the and controllers. So before moving on, create a first and explore which logic is shared between them:

Then update the with this:

It has way less lines of code now and it holds only the logic specific for the carousel feature.

So with this knowledge, and the HTML already done, the controller will be pretty straightforward, but still fairly involved:

First off: there are a few extra values set. Then the method is overwritten (which is called in the method in ).

In the method the check for a valid swipe is ran. If valid, it fires . Here it check swipe distance: if a positive integer, run and otherwise . In both methods would you use the Fetch API to make a request to your back-end (see this article how you can make requests in Stimulus controllers). Here I have stubbed it with a simple .

Then finally, again in both methods, the is ran that takes care of the transitioning of the element off the screen and then removing it from the DOM.

Let's add one more nicety to the swipe-card controller, when the event is fired, lets add a little CSS to the cards for some fun. Add touchmove->swipe-card#touchMove to the element's . The add the following method to the controller:

This will move and slightly rotate the element based on the horizontal distance () from where the touch/drag started. Smooth!

And that is how I approach Stimulus controllers with similar functionality. Got suggestions or ideas to make this better? Let me know. 🤙
